title: Rubric-based behavioral evaluation framework wired into the loop as an advisory gate
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 7. basicly has deterministic gates + advisory semantic review but no use-case-tied yes/no behavioral rubrics. Add catalog-authored rubric sources evaluated deterministic-where-possible else agent-judged, reported as an advisory gate promotable per work-type. See design proposal in docs/foundry-spike.md dimension 7.

## Acceptance Criteria

- New catalog source .basicly/core/rubrics/*.rubric.yaml (id, description, applies_to work-types, checks[] each kind deterministic|judged), authored as YAML and loaded/validated/linted like other catalog sources; sample rubrics ship
- basicly rubric eval <issue> selects the rubric by the bead work-type, runs deterministic checks via the verify runner (exit code answers yes/no) and dispatches judged checks via a review-style prompt through the agent-agnostic runner (handoff when no CLI), producing structured yes/no plus evidence verdicts
- The command emits a rubric gate via br gate report, non-required (advisory) by default and promotable via [policy] required_gates
- Tests cover source load/lint, work-type selection, deterministic eval with real exit codes, judged eval with a mocked/handoff runner, and gate emission; judged verdicts stay advisory (live-agent answer unverified, same bar as catalog review)
- pytest, ruff, pyright, and the projection gates stay green
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Verify hook stages beyond pre-commit and rule on the config filename
description:
Investigate whether the shipped hooks really cover more than the pre-commit stage (expected: pre-commit, commit-msg, pre-push — and whether post-commit/post-push are wanted). Verify against hooks.yaml and the installed git hooks. Then rule on the user's suggestion to genericize the .pre-commit-config.yaml filename (e.g. .hook-config.yaml) — note the filename is mandated by the pre-commit framework itself, so document the finding instead if a rename is impossible.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A verified list of which git stages the catalog ships hooks for, from hooks.yaml and pre-commit install stages
- An explicit answer on renaming .pre-commit-config.yaml, with rationale
- Docs updated where the pre-commit-only impression is created
close_reason: three git stages plus a claude agent hook verified; rename rejected (framework-mandated filename), readme clarified

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Verified: hooks.yaml ships pre-commit (identity-guard, pre-commit-script, catalog-lint), commit-msg (commit-msg-script, beads-commit-msg-script), pre-push (pre-push-script), and a Claude PreToolUse agent hook (protect-generated); all three git stages are installed in .git/hooks and every config entry carries an explicit stages: list. Ruling on the rename: .pre-commit-config.yaml is the pre-commit FRAMEWORK's mandated discovery filename — the tool manages every git stage despite its name, and a rename to .hook-config.yaml would break the tool and its editor/CI integrations. No rename; the README wiring line now spells this out. post-commit is supported by the framework but cannot block (the commit already exists), and git has no client-side post-push hook at all — so no after-commit/after-push hooks are warranted.

title: scaffold vscode tasks for build, update and uninstall on install
description:
VS Code users need one-click harness operations after editing their overlay: rebuild projections (the headline task), skills/hooks rebuilds, upgrade, and removal. Scaffold a .vscode/tasks.json during basicly install — never overwriting an existing file — with single-command tasks using the uvx git+ channel (no shell && chaining; must work in PowerShell 5, cmd, bash).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Fresh install writes .vscode/tasks.json with tasks: "basicly: build" (fragment projections), "basicly: skills-build", "basicly: hooks-build", "basicly: update" (install/upgrade converge), "basicly: uninstall"; each is a single cross-platform shell command via uvx --from git+https://github.com/niksavis/basicly@main.

title: Identity: stamp the dispatched agent (and model) as attribution on landed commits and gate results
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 1. Commits and gates carry only the human git identity; the runner name/model is known at dispatch and discarded (includeCoAuthoredBy is false). The D3 run-record (.basicly/usage/run-records.json) already holds agent+model per bead; this bead surfaces that provenance as attribution on the landed commit and the gate result.

## Acceptance Criteria

- run_record gains a latest_record(repo_root, bead_id) -> RunRecord | None accessor returning the most recent record for the bead (None when absent or empty).
- The landing merge commit carries agent attribution as git trailers sourced from the latest run-record: Harness-Runner: <agent>, plus Harness-Model: <model> when the run pinned one. No record -> the merge message is unchanged. The conventional subject and the trailing bead id are preserved so the commit-msg and beads hooks still pass.
- The verify gate records the runner: br gate report gets --actor <agent> and the model folded into the note, sourced from the latest run-record, for both the loop landing (loop._record_verify) and basicly verify --issue. No record -> no actor and the note is unchanged.
- Graceful when no run-record exists (verify run outside a dispatch): nothing breaks, no attribution added.
- Regression tests: the latest_record accessor; the merge message with and without a record; report_gate passing actor/model; the loop wiring reads the record.
- Architecture section 12.8 (and the dimension-1 attribution note) updated.
- pytest, ruff, and the projection checks stay green.

## Scope

Attribution from the existing run-record only: a merge-commit trailer and a gate actor. No per-agent bot identity (basicly-smzg), no commit signing or tamper-evidence, no new attribution source. Trailer chosen over a synthetic Co-Authored-By (no fake identity/email) per the and/or in the spike recommendation.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Design: land after ship approval and fold the bead close into the merge commit
description:
Follow-up design track from basicly-flp. Today the loop lands a worktree at the build->verify transition, BEFORE the verify phase gate and the human ship checkpoint, and closes the bead in a separate post-ship tracker-only commit; merge_worktree also verifies twice (in the worktree post-rebase, then again on base via _record_verify, an identical tree). Proposed redesign: reorder to build -> verify (gate recorded from the worktree run) -> ship checkpoint -> land+close in one step, where the merge uses git merge --no-ff --no-commit, then closes the bead, flushes, stages .beads/issues.jsonl, and commits - one tracker-carrying merge commit per track, nothing lands on main before gates and human approval, and the duplicate base re-verify disappears. Must be designed against: feature fan-in (children land through the queue when all closed - their self-landing order changes), rework semantics after a landing failure, multi-track concurrency (base working-tree jsonl carrying another track's uncommitted claim at merge time), and loop_state phase derivation (verified currently implies a bound worktree + green gates; landing evidence moves). See the investigation and decision comments on basicly-flp.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A design comment (or architecture.md section update) covers phase reorder, fan-in, rework, concurrency, and phase-derivation impacts with a go/no-go recommendation; if go, implementation is decomposed into child beads. Full gate suite passes for anything landed.

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Scoped down by basicly-c9e5 (br redirect worktrees + engine-owned tracker commits): once c9e5 ships, the residual value here is governance only - landing before ship approval, and folding the close into the merge commit saves one CI-free engine commit. Re-evaluate go/no-go after c9e5.

title: single-source scoped rules: stop emitting .github/instructions by default
description:
VS Code loads both .claude/rules/*.md and .github/instructions/*.instructions.md with no dedup — it name-dedupes only skills (user-shared findings from a separate research session, 2026-07-16; metadata-alignment hypotheses were tested and disproved there). Path-scoped guidance therefore double-loads in VS Code for every consumer. That session adopted single-source emission (scoped rules to .claude/rules only, .github/instructions twins swept, a verifier failing if they reappear) — a basicly install refresh in a repo with that policy would regress it, so this must land before the next consumer refresh.

Change the default projection so scoped rules emit to .claude/rules/ only:
- copilot target keeps .github/copilot-instructions.md (root trio unchanged — each file has a distinct native reader)
- skills stay dual-emitted (.claude/skills + .agents/skills): Claude reads only the former, Codex only the latter, and VS Code safely name-dedupes them
- install/uninstall sweep previously manifest-tracked .github/instructions/*.instructions.md files in consumers
- trade-off, accepted: github.com-side Copilot (PR code review, cloud agent) loses path-scoped rules and keeps only the root copilot-instructions.md

## Acceptance Criteria
- basicly build/install no longer emits .github/instructions/*.instructions.md; basicly's own two files (python-style, test-discipline) are swept from .github/instructions/
- .github/copilot-instructions.md, AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, and .claude/rules/*.md output is unchanged
- skills remain dual-emitted to .claude/skills and .agents/skills
- a consumer re-running basicly install has its stale .github/instructions projections removed (manifest-driven)
- docs/architecture.md targets section records the single-source policy and the github.com trade-off
- tests cover the removed target and the stale-file sweep

title: Guardrails: runner output secret-redaction plus egress-policy seam
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 2. Captured runner stdout/stderr is never inspected/filtered/egress-limited. Redact secrets from persisted/surfaced runner output and optionally assert a network-restricted sandbox for headless runs.

Investigation (2026-07-18): run_record.py already never persists output (only a redacted command), so the gap is the surfaced/live RunResult.stdout/stderr, emitted raw by the CLI runner run and read by the loop/rubrics. The yzyd secret-scan hook (.basicly/core/hooks/secret-scan.py) has a high-signal pattern set to reuse, but it is a standalone stdlib hook (not importable), so a sibling redact module in src/basicly mirrors its patterns. Egress: basicly cannot portably network-sandbox a generic subprocess; that is agent-layer (codex sandbox = basicly-t0kt, claude/copilot config), so this task documents the egress posture rather than building a speculative seam (user-scoped 2026-07-18).

## Acceptance Criteria

- A new redact module redacts high-signal secret patterns (private-key header, aws/github/slack/google/openai tokens, slack webhook, and the generic secret-named assignment with placeholder filtering) from arbitrary text, replacing each hit with a labeled placeholder; secret-free text is returned unchanged.
- runner.run applies redaction to captured stdout and stderr so every surfaced/persisted RunResult carries redacted output (guardrail at the source); dry-run and handoff results (empty output) are unaffected.
- The redaction patterns mirror the secret-scan hook (basicly-yzyd) as the sibling detector, noted in a comment so the two stay in step.
- Egress is documented as delegated to the agent-layer sandbox (codex basicly-t0kt, claude/copilot config); basicly redacts output but does not sandbox a generic subprocess. No speculative seam is added.
- Deterministic tests cover each secret pattern class, secret-free passthrough, and run() redacting both stdout and stderr.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Inference layer: first-class model field on runner adapters with recorded provenance
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 4. RunnerSpec has no model field; you can only hand-encode --model in argv. Add an optional model field to RunnerSpec/[[runner.agents]] rendered into the command template (later per-track model choice) and record model provenance via the D3 run-record.

- The run-record stamps model provenance per dispatch (`build_record` threads `spec.model`; `loop._record_run` passes it), replacing the reserved null.
- `runner list` and `runner dry-run` surface the pinned model.
- Architecture section 12.8 and the runner/config sample comments are updated.

title: wire markdownlint-cli2 into pre-commit and CI; fix existing findings
description:
markdownlint-cli2 is a devDependency with a root .markdownlint-cli2.yaml, and CI already provisions Node 22 + npm install, but nothing ever invokes the linter — the only judge is the VS Code extension, so violations accumulate silently (38 found on 2026-07-15 in docs/architecture.md alone after markdownlint v0.41 added MD060). Wire it as a deterministic gate and fix existing findings. Generated projections (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, copilot-instructions.md, skills, rules/instructions) are generator-owned and must be ignored by the lint config so the gate never fights the projector (their lint-cleanliness is a separate projector concern).

## Acceptance Criteria

- markdownlint-cli2 runs as a pre-commit hook (foreign/local block, cross-platform entry) and as a quality-gates CI step; both use the repo config.
- .markdownlint-cli2.yaml ignores generated outputs (AGENTS.md, .claude/CLAUDE.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, projected skills/rules/instructions trees).
- Existing violations in hand-authored markdown are fixed (docs/architecture.md MD060/MD049 auto-fix; the line-714 accidental "+"-list unwrap).
- Repo-wide markdownlint run exits 0; a commit touching markdown triggers the hook.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: DoR: accept the structured br acceptance-criteria field instead of forcing a body heading
description: Surfaced while shipping basicly-shgo (2026-07-18): br has a structured acceptance_criteria field (settable via 'br update --acceptance-criteria' queryable via 'br show --json'), but basicly policy.definition_of_ready is a thin wrapper over 'br lint --json' which checks the description BODY for template sections (the Acceptance Criteria heading) not the structured field. Consequence: to pass the decompose Definition-of-Ready gate acceptance criteria must be duplicated into the body even after being set in the field. Proven: field-only was blocked by DoR; adding the body heading unblocked it. Design open for the loop. Note 'br lint' checks MORE than AC (e.g. Steps to Reproduce for bugs) so this is not a clean swap - a likely direction is to treat a non-empty acceptance_criteria field as SATISFYING the AC template requirement while preserving other template checks. Investigate whether beads_rust lint can read the field before adding a basicly-side workaround.
acceptance_criteria: policy.definition_of_ready accepts a non-empty structured acceptance_criteria field as satisfying the AC requirement without a body heading; other br lint template checks (e.g. bug Steps to Reproduce) preserved; regression test covers field-set-body-empty passing DoR and neither-set failing; decision recorded on whether the fix lives upstream in beads_rust lint config or in basicly definition_of_ready.

description:
A9: support basicly.local.toml (gitignored) overlaying basicly.toml for per-machine harness sections (runner, worktree, verify, policy); install scaffolds mention it and report config sections present in the shipped default but missing from the consumer file.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Values in basicly.local.toml override basicly.toml for harness config; projection config stays repo-level; the file is gitignored by scaffolding; install prints missing-section hints without editing the existing file. Tests cover precedence. Full gate suite passes.

title: Committer uv diagnostic and docs
description:
A11: rendered git hooks assume uv on PATH for every committer. Add an explicit committer-requirements note (README + CONTRIBUTING) and a hooks-check warning when uv is absent at check time so the failure is diagnosed before commit time.

## Acceptance Criteria

- hooks-check warns clearly when uv is missing; docs state that every committer to a basicly-managed repo needs uv (and python 3.14+). Full gate suite passes.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Windows: deterministic rubric check test mangles the executable path via posix shlex
description:
tests/test_rubrics.py::test_evaluate_deterministic_maps_exit_code fails on windows-latest CI. evaluate_deterministic splits the check command with shlex.split (posix), and the test embeds sys.executable, which on Windows is a backslash path (D:\a\...\python.exe). POSIX shlex treats backslash as an escape and mangles the path, so python is not found, the check exits non-zero, and the YES assertion fails. It is deterministic on Windows, not flaky.

## Steps to Reproduce

- Run pytest on Windows: tests/test_rubrics.py::test_evaluate_deterministic_maps_exit_code.
- The ok command {sys.executable} -c pass shlex-splits to a mangled path and exits non-zero.

## Acceptance Criteria

- The test builds the python path with forward slashes (Path(sys.executable).as_posix()) so posix shlex.split does not mangle it and Windows still accepts the executable path; both the YES and NO assertions hold on Windows and POSIX.
- No production change: realistic authored rubric commands use bare executables or forward-slash paths; a comment records why the test uses as_posix.
- pytest stays green on all platforms.
close_reason: fixed: forward-slash executable path; windows-latest CI green (run 29640697833)

title: Speed up quality-gates CI: parallelize pytest with xdist and drop the duplicate pre-commit step
description:
The Windows quality-gates job ran ~2m47s vs ubuntu ~1m04s. Root cause: 94 subprocess-spawning CLI tests (cold python + jinja2/rich import per spawn) dominate on Windows, and CI ran the deterministic gates twice (pre-commit fast + pre-push full; full is a superset).

Fix:
1. Add pytest-xdist dev dep; run the repo pytest gate with -n auto (basicly.toml verify command). Local parallel run: 483 tests 27s -> 5.2s. Suite audited xdist-safe (all mutation in tmp_path/work_repo; REPO_ROOT reads are read-only).
2. Remove the redundant "Run pre-commit checks" CI step (full pre-push supersets it).

## Acceptance Criteria

- pytest runs in parallel via the repo gate; quality-gates.yml runs the full gate once; full suite green; Windows gate wall time materially reduced.
close_reason: Shipped: pytest-xdist -n auto via the repo gate; dropped the duplicate pre-commit CI step. Landed 27601fd, on origin/main.

title: architecture.md: keep implementation notes only for unimplemented parts
description:
Sweep docs/architecture.md: sections that are shipped should read as description of what exists (no implementation-gap/plan language); only genuinely unimplemented parts keep NOT-implemented markers.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Every shipped capability is described in present tense as existing behavior, with no residual "gap"/"planned"/"will be" phrasing.
- Only genuinely unimplemented items (currently: Codex scoped-rules renderer, Cursor target renderer) carry explicit not-implemented markers.
- Statements verified against the codebase before rewording; no invented capabilities.
- markdownlint and repo gates pass.

title: Wire the selected runner into the loop build phase (auto-dispatch)
description:
Follow-up to onb.7 (architecture §12.8). The runner abstraction + CLI (basicly runner list/dry-run/run) exist; wire runner.select_runner + runner.run into loop._start_build_leaf so the loop auto-dispatches a node's coding to the selected agent headless in its worktree (handoff runner leaves the existing 'awaiting the agent's work' block intact). Keep the loop agent-neutral.

## Acceptance Criteria
- With a headless runner selected (config default or auto detection), `basicly loop advance` on a ready leaf provisions the worktree and dispatches the agent headless inside it, then blocks reporting the run outcome
- With the manual/handoff runner selected, behavior is unchanged: the loop blocks with the existing awaiting-the-agents-work message
- A failed headless run blocks with the runner name and exit code instead of pretending the work happened
- The dispatch prompt is agent-neutral: it names the bead id, points at AGENTS.md and `br show`, and forbids merging/pushing/closing (the loop owns landing)
- Regression tests cover the handoff, success, and failure dispatch paths

title: prune invalid write/multiedit/notebookedit permission deny rules
description: Claude Code startup warns that Write/MultiEdit/NotebookEdit permission deny rules are invalid (only Edit(path) file rules match; MultiEdit is not a known tool). A prior session added these variants to permissions.yaml env-edit on the mistaken assumption Edit left a write path open. Reduce to Edit(.env*) only, prune stale settings.json entries, drop MultiEdit from the default hook matcher, and fix the regression test.

title: scaffold a consumer ci gates workflow on install
description:
Consumers get local git-hook gates from install but no CI floor: nothing validates commit messages, drift, or the configured verify checks on push/PR. Ship a curated consumer workflow scaffolded by install (never clobbering an existing file), stack-agnostic like the rest of the consumer surface.

## Acceptance Criteria

- install scaffolds .github/workflows/basicly-gates.yml when absent (note printed when present; file is the user's afterwards) with: (1) commit-message validation over the push/PR range via the core commit-msg + beads-commit-msg hooks, (2) basicly check (projection drift), (3) basicly verify --mode full (configured checks; empty passes) — basicly invoked via uvx --from git+https://github.com/niksavis/basicly@main.
- The workflow assumes no consumer stack beyond git + uv on the runner.
- uninstall --purge removes the workflow only when byte-identical to the scaffold.
- Tests cover scaffold, no-clobber, and purge paths; workflow YAML parses (yaml.safe_load in a test).
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: catalog-lint hook fails in consumers: engine not importable
description:
Found live during the terminal acceptance (basicly-zrj.15): the projected catalog-lint hook runs [sys.executable, -m, basicly.cli, catalog-lint], which requires the basicly package importable in the consumer venv — true only in the authoring repo (and the old vendored-engine era). In a real consumer the hook fails every commit with ModuleNotFoundError.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. basicly install in a consumer repo (hooks activate; catalog-lint wired via .pre-commit-config.yaml).
2. git commit: catalog-lint hook exits 1 — "No module named 'basicly'" (observed in terminal).

## Acceptance Criteria

- The hook resolves the CLI via a ladder: basicly on PATH -> python -m basicly.cli (importable) -> uvx --from the pinned git+ source; when none is available it prints an actionable warning and passes (advisory) instead of hard-failing consumer commits.
- The scaffolded consumer CI workflow (basicly-gates.yml) gains a catalog-lint step as the deterministic backstop.
- Tests cover the importable path and the none-available advisory skip.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Rewrite core AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md/copilot-instructions.md fragments for genericity and signal
description: Researched external agent-instruction repos (lattice, gastownhall/beads, JuliusBrussee skills, mattpocock/skills, ponytail) and redesigned the .basicly/core/fragments catalog: moved repo-specific content (project-defaults, python-style) into .basicly-local overlay, rewrote weak fragments (core-rules, quality-gate, if-stuck->decision-protocol, no-user-path-leakage->secure-coding) with concrete, high-signal guidance, added new generic fragments (knowledge-priming, session-completion, non-interactive-shell). Also fixed a real bug: applyTo in generated copilot instructions must be a string not a YAML array. Removed redundant source-fragments comment from generated file headers (context poisoning with no tooling use).
close_reason: Fragment catalog redesigned and committed in 0220a35: core fragments genericized, weak content rewritten, applyTo bug fixed.

title: Refresh architecture.md to the current reality
description: Sweep docs/architecture.md for statements that lag the shipped state (post v0.1.3: technology scoping, runner auto-dispatch, bootstrap shims, agents kind) and update stale Planned/Partial/Deferred markers, examples, and section cross-references.
close_reason: stale Partial/Planned markers fixed (3.3 verification, zrj.13 robustness, v0.1.0 release item); aff/7ca/hmc sections were already refreshed when they shipped

title: adopt agent-file SOTA findings: descriptive blocks, tool-time gates, deny gaps
description:
Research (2026-07-15, background agent over 9 web sources incl. Anthropic best-practices/memory/hooks docs, agents.md spec, GitHub Copilot docs, plus awesome-claude-md's 104 curated analyses and awesome-claude-code cloned locally) identified the canonical building blocks of state-of-the-art agent instruction files and control mechanisms, compared against this catalog.

Verdict: basicly is unusually STRONG on process blocks (quality-gate, git-discipline, harness-loop workflow, decision-protocol — ahead of the curated corpus) and its determinism split (git hooks + deny-list + catalog lint) is directionally validated by Anthropic's own advisory-vs-enforced distinction. It is unusually WEAK on the descriptive blocks every source puts first: (1) project overview/purpose+stack, (2) fenced build/test/lint commands — both effectively missing from the core scaffold — plus (3) no tool-time (PreToolUse) hook projection, (4) a .env Edit/Write deny gap (only Read is denied), (5) no quirks/incident category for retro output, (6) vague-adjective core-rules bullets that fail Anthropic's deletion test, (7) zero scarce-emphasis markers.

Key phrasing rules to adopt in authoring guidance: concrete+verifiable beats vague; commands in code fences are run verbatim, prose commands are improvised; pair every prohibition with an alternative; give rationale so rules generalize; emphasis is a scarce resource; every rule traces to a real incident; the deletion test ("would removing this cause mistakes? if not, cut").

## Acceptance Criteria

- Each child bead lands or is explicitly rejected with a recorded reason.
- Catalog-authoring guidance absorbs the phrasing rules (fenced commands, prohibition+alternative, rationale clauses, scarce emphasis, deletion test).
close_reason: done

comments.author: niksa
comments.text:
# State of the Art: Coding-Agent Instruction Files & Control Mechanisms

Research base: 9 web sources (3 primary/official, 6 secondary) + 2 curated GitHub collections cloned and analyzed locally (104 curated CLAUDE.md analyses in awesome-claude-md; ~40 relevant control-tooling entries in awesome-claude-code), compared against basicly's 19 fragment sources (17 core + 2 local overlay), 6 git-hook gates, and `.claude/settings.json`.

---

## 1. Canonical building blocks table

| # | Block | Purpose | Evidence | basicly coverage |
|---|-------|---------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Project overview + tech stack** ("Project overview" — agents.md; "WHAT/WHY" — HumanLayer; "high-level summary, languages, frameworks" — GitHub) | 1-2 line purpose + stack with versions; what the repo is *for* | agents.md spec, GitHub Copilot docs, DataCamp, HumanLayer, awesome-claude-md (architecture blocks in ~98/109 analyses) | **WEAK** — `project-defaults` (local overlay only, 6 bullets; states stack but no purpose/"why", and it isn't a core scaffolded block) |
| 2 | **Build/test/lint commands** ("Commands" — DataCamp; "bash commands Claude can't guess" — Anthropic; "build/test/validation steps with specific command sequences" — GitHub) | Fenced, verbatim-runnable, grouped by purpose (Setup/Dev/Test/Build) | Every source; awesome-claude-md rates it 2nd-most-universal (~80/109); GitHub: "validate each command by running it" | **MISSING** — `project-defaults` explicitly says "No global build/test command yet"; per-tool skills (`tool-uv`) exist but there is no commands block, and nothing in code fences |
| 3 | **Architecture / directory map** ("Directory Map" — DataCamp; "file-to-function mapping" — Overreacted; "packages/* mapped to purpose" — Cloudflare) | Top-level layout → responsibility; data flow; entry points | DataCamp, GitHub Copilot, awesome-claude-md near-universal. *Countervailing primary evidence*: Anthropic's `/doctor` trim check cuts "directory layouts, dependency lists, and architecture overviews" as derivable — keep only what Claude can't infer | **MISSING** (docs/architecture.md exists but nothing points at it from the always-on file) |
| 4 | **Code style — deltas from defaults only** | Only conventions that differ from tool/language defaults; delegate the rest to formatters | Anthropic include/exclude table ("code style rules that differ from defaults" vs "standard language conventions Claude already knows"); HumanLayer ("never send an LLM to do a linter's job"); awesome-claude-md ~66/109 | **COVERED** — `python-style` (path-scoped rule, 3 bullets, points at `ruff format` pre-commit hook). Model implementation |
| 5 | **Testing instructions/philosophy** | Test runner, how to run one test, isolation rules, what to assert on | agents.md example sections, Anthropic table, awesome-claude-md ~68/109 ("tiered testing", Callstack "philosophy" framing) | **COVERED** — `test-discipline` (path-scoped). Missing only the *runner command* (see block 2) |
| 6 | **Repo etiquette / git & PR workflow** ("Repository etiquette" — Anthropic; "PR instructions" — agents.md) | Commit format, branch naming, PR conventions, hook interaction | Anthropic, agents.md, GitHub Copilot, awesome-claude-md (~22/109 quality-gate/commit blocks) | **STRONG** — `git-discipline` + `conventional-commits` skill + commit-msg hooks; the point-at-the-gate pattern here is ahead of most curated examples |
| 7 | **Boundaries / never-do list / protected paths** ("DO NOT EDIT generated files" — Pydantic; "Use pnpm — never npm or yarn" — Cloudflare) | Hard prohibitions with alternatives; generated-file and tool mandates | awesome-claude-md ~31/109; DataCamp (negation + alternative); GitHub ("prescriptive language") | **PARTIAL** — `require-explicit-confirmation` is a *confirmation* list, not a never-do list; several items are already deterministically denied in settings.json (overlap unstated). No "generated files — edit fragments instead" warning on the generated CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md themselves |
| 8 | **Verification / definition of done** ("Give Claude a check it can run" — Anthropic) | A pass/fail signal Claude runs itself; evidence over assertion | Anthropic best-practices (its top tip), awesome-claude-md quality-gate blocks, Kent Beck handoff example | **STRONG** — `quality-gate` (6 bullets; arguably the densest fragment; one of the best instances of this block found anywhere) |
| 9 | **Quirks / gotchas / environment setup** ("Quirks" — DataCamp; "document workarounds and misbehavior encountered" — GitHub; "common gotchas or non-obvious behaviors" — Anthropic) | Undocumented traps: env vars, timing, import order, platform issues | DataCamp, GitHub Copilot, Anthropic include-table, ayautomate ("gotcha-forward") | **WEAK** — `non-interactive-shell` is the only gotcha-type fragment; no per-repo quirks block, and no category for retro output to land in |
| 10 | **Workflow / methodology protocol** (plan-then-code; RIPER-5 modes; TDD contract — Kent Beck) | Phased workflow with allowed actions per phase and enforced checkpoints | Anthropic (explore→plan→implement→commit), awesome-claude-md Tier-1/2 picks (GoMall, Kent Beck) | **STRONG** — `harness-loop` with engine-enforced checkpoints; goes further than any curated example (they enforce via prose; basicly enforces via engine) |
| 11 | **Progressive-disclosure index / pointers** ("Prefer pointers to copies" — HumanLayer; "reference, don't embed" — DataCamp) | Where skills/rules/docs live; load-on-demand instead of inline | HumanLayer, DataCamp, Anthropic memory docs (skills vs rules vs CLAUDE.md decision), Copilot `applyTo` | **COVERED** — `use`, `claude-defaults`, `copilot-defaults`, `catalog-authoring`; skills carry tool knowledge |
| 12 | **Escalation / decision protocol** | When to act autonomously vs stop and ask | Weakest external evidence — appears only implicitly (plan mode, RIPER-5 mode permissions). Largely a basicly original; flag as opinion-supported, not SOTA-corroborated | **COVERED** — `decision-protocol` (basicly is ahead of the corpus here) |

---

## 2. Top 10 phrasing rules (best vs worst)

1. **Concrete and verifiable beats vague intent.** Best: "Use 2-space indentation, no semicolons, single quotes." Worst: "Format code properly." (Anthropic memory docs + DataCamp, verbatim-corroborated.)
2. **Put runnable commands in code fences.** "A command inside a code fence is something Claude will run verbatim. A command written in a sentence is a suggestion it might improvise on." (DataCamp.) Worst: prose like "run the linter before committing" with no command.
3. **Pair every prohibition with an alternative.** Best: "Never use `--legacy-peer-deps`; resolve conflicts by updating the package." Worst: negation-only rules that leave the agent stuck. (DataCamp; basicly already does this well: "…bypassing hooks — fix the failing gate instead.")
4. **Give rationale so the rule generalizes.** Best: "Never force push. This rewrites shared history and is unrecoverable for collaborators." Worst: bare "Never force push." (DataCamp.)
5. **Emphasis is a scarce resource.** Anthropic: "You can tune instructions by adding emphasis (e.g., 'IMPORTANT' or 'YOU MUST')" — but "if every rule is marked important, the emphasis becomes invisible" (DataCamp). Worst: bolding everything. (Note: basicly currently uses *zero* emphasis markers — headroom exists.)
6. **Strict imperative mandates for critical tool choices, with reasoning.** Best: "Use `pnpm` — never use npm or yarn" (Cloudflare); "UV over pip, basedpyright over mypy" with stated rationale (Pydantic). Worst: "use appropriate tooling."
7. **Point at the gate, don't restate it.** Best: "Commits are gated by commit-msg hooks — use the `conventional-commits` skill" (basicly's own pattern, matching HumanLayer/DataCamp "reference, don't embed"). Worst: restating linter rules the formatter already enforces.
8. **Only repo-specific content.** "Every line should answer 'what does Claude need to know about THIS repo?'" (ayautomate). Worst: "TypeScript is a typed superset of JavaScript"; "write clean code" (Anthropic's explicit exclude list).
9. **Every rule traces to a real incident.** "Every rule should trace back to a real incident, not a hypothetical one" (DataCamp); identity-guard.py's docstring ("the exact failure this repo hit") is a textbook instance. Worst: speculative edge-case rules that "help in narrow situations and hurt in the majority of sessions."
10. **The deletion test.** "Would removing this cause Claude to make mistakes? If not, cut it" (Anthropic + DataCamp, identical phrasing). Corollary anti-pattern: "If Claude keeps ignoring a rule, don't add more words around it" — the file is too long. Sources converge on <200 lines (Anthropic), <300/"under sixty" (HumanLayer), 100-400 (ayautomate); GitHub caps Copilot instructions at "no longer than 2 pages."

---

## 3. Determinism ledger

The operating principle under test — *everything that doesn't have to be suggestive should be deterministic and gated* — is **directly validated by primary sources**: Anthropic's memory doc says "Claude treats them as context, not enforced configuration. To block an action regardless of what Claude decides, use a PreToolUse hook instead," and its managed-settings table splits "technical enforcement" (settings) from "behavioral guidance" (CLAUDE.md). Anthropic's own steering post is "organized around deterministic-versus-probabilistic control" (awesome-claude-code, From Anthropic section). awesome-claude-md's meta-lesson: "enforce standards through tooling rather than relying on developer discipline."

| Concern | Recommended mechanism | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Formatting, import order, lint | Formatter + pre-commit/PostToolUse hook; **never prose** | HumanLayer, DataCamp, awesome-claude-md (unanimous) |
| Commit message format | commit-msg hook | awesome-claude-md quality-gate examples; basicly practice |
| Protected files (.env, lockfiles, generated, migrations) | PreToolUse hook (exit 2) or `permissions.deny` | Anthropic hooks guide (protect-files.sh example, "Write a hook that blocks writes to the migrations folder") |
| Destructive commands (rm -rf, force-push, DROP TABLE) | `permissions.deny` + PreToolUse guardrail script; a whole tool ecosystem exists (Claude Code Safety Net, Safety Guard "born from a real incident", GouvernAI "auto-approve safe, gate risky, block dangerous", Node9) | Anthropic hooks guide (block-rm-rf.sh, "dropping tables is not allowed" exit-2 example); awesome-claude-code Security category |
| Actions that must happen every time | Hooks: "for actions that must happen every time with zero exceptions. Unlike CLAUDE.md instructions which are advisory, hooks are deterministic" | Anthropic best-practices, verbatim |
| Definition-of-done checks | Stop hook ("blocks the turn from ending until it passes") or CI | Anthropic best-practices |
| Timed/positional instructions ("before every commit") | Hook, not CLAUDE.md: "if the instruction must run at a specific point… write it as a hook instead" | Anthropic memory docs, verbatim |
| Org-wide policy | Managed settings `permissions.deny` / `sandbox.enabled` for enforcement; managed CLAUDE.md only for guidance | Anthropic memory docs table |
| Secret leakage | Scanner in git hooks/CI (Agent Guard), not prose | awesome-claude-code Security |
| Autonomy blast radius | OS sandbox / Docker isolation ("give the agent a cage, not your keys" — Cleat) + `--allowedTools` scoping | Anthropic best-practices, awesome-claude-code |
| Instruction-file quality itself | Lint it: agnix (linter/LSP for CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md/hooks), Schliff (8-dimension scorer) — validates basicly's `catalog-lint` approach | awesome-claude-code Linting category |
| What stays prose | Judgment calls: architecture rationale, escalation rules, review philosophy, style *deltas*, gotchas — anything requiring context-sensitive judgment ("prompt-based hooks" exist for the middle ground) | Anthropic hooks guide |

---

## 4. Prioritized recommendations for basicly's catalog

1. **(High) Add a first-class `project-overview` + `commands` core scaffold.** The two most-corroborated blocks (purpose/stack, fenced commands) are the weakest in basicly. `project-defaults` is a thin local overlay with no "why" and an explicit "no build/test command" placeholder. Concrete: make `basicly init`/docs require a per-repo `project-overview` fragment (purpose in 1-2 lines, stack with versions, entry points, pointer to `docs/architecture.md`) and a `commands` fragment whose body is fenced command groups (`uv sync`, `uv run pytest`, `basicly catalog-lint`, hook build). Body sketch for commands: "```\nuv run pytest              # full suite\nuv run pytest tests/x.py   # single file\nbasicly catalog-lint       # catalog format gate\n```". Heed the `/doctor` caveat: pointers to architecture docs, not embedded layouts.
2. **(High) Close the deny-list gap on `.env` and demote confirmed-deterministic prose.** settings.json denies `Read(.env*)` but **not `Edit`/`Write`** on them — the confirmation-list item "editing … `.env*`" is enforceable today (`Edit(.env*)`, `Write(.env*)` deny rules, or a projected protect-files PreToolUse hook). Then annotate `require-explicit-confirmation` to state which items are hard-denied (force-push, `--no-verify`, `rm -rf`, `reset --hard`) so prose carries rationale, and only the genuinely judgment-y items (dependency changes, CI/CD edits, new network calls) remain suggestive.
3. **(High) Project agent-native hooks, not just git hooks.** basicly's targets project markdown + git hooks; git hooks gate only at commit time. Anthropic's model gates at *tool* time (PreToolUse) and *turn end* (Stop). Concrete: add a `targets/*.yaml` output that renders a hooks manifest into `.claude/settings.json` (and Copilot equivalents as they mature) — starting with a protect-files guard for generated outputs (`CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `copilot-instructions.md` — "DO NOT EDIT generated files" is a Tier-1 curated pattern (Pydantic), and basicly's equivalent rule currently lives only in `catalog-authoring` prose).
4. **(Medium) Add a `quirks` fragment category and wire the retro to it.** DataCamp's "every rule traces to a real incident" is exactly `self-improvement-retro`'s philosophy, but retro proposals have no natural landing zone for repo-specific traps (the identity-guard hook's origin story is the pattern). A `quirks/` category with one-incident-one-bullet discipline completes the loop.
5. **(Medium) Prune `core-rules` against the exclude-list.** Several bullets fail the deletion test per Anthropic's exclude table ("self-evident practices like 'write clean code'"): "Prioritize correctness over speed" and "Prefer explicit, readable solutions over clever ones" are vague-adjective rules the model follows anyway. The generated file is 116 lines (healthy, under the 200-line consensus) — but the just-completed size-cap commit suggests budget pressure; these are the cheapest cuts.
6. **(Low) Introduce scarce emphasis.** Zero `IMPORTANT`/`YOU MUST` markers exist across the catalog. Anthropic explicitly endorses sparse emphasis for adherence tuning; consider it for exactly one rule per projection (candidate: the never-bypass-gates rule, which prose must still carry for agents other than Claude Code).
7. **(Low, flag-as-opinion) Add rationale clauses to bare prohibitions.** e.g. secure-coding's "Never commit secrets" bullets already carry alternatives; `require-explicit-confirmation`'s "Force-push, history rewrite" line would generalize better with a one-clause "rewrites shared history" rationale (single-source DataCamp claim, uncorroborated by primary docs).

**Overall verdict:** basicly's fragment set is unusually strong on process blocks (verification, git etiquette, workflow protocol, decision rules — better than the curated corpus) and unusually weak on the *descriptive* blocks every source puts first (what the project is, how to build/test it, where things live). Its determinism split is directionally correct and ahead of most examples (git-hook gates + deny-list + catalog lint), with the two concrete deterministic gaps being tool-time hooks and the `.env` write hole.

---

## 5. Sources

| Source | Credibility |
|---|---|
| Anthropic — Claude Code best practices (code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices) | Primary/official; the include/exclude table and hooks-vs-advisory distinction are the strongest evidence in this report. |
| Anthropic — Memory docs (code.claude.com/docs/en/memory) | Primary/official; canonical on locations, imports, rules, size (<200 lines), and the enforcement split (managed settings vs CLAUDE.md). |
| Anthropic — Hooks guide (code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide) | Primary/official; canonical on PreToolUse blocking (exit 2 / JSON deny), protect-files and block-rm-rf examples. |
| agents.md spec | Primary for the AGENTS.md convention (60k+ repos, 20+ tools); deliberately minimal — suggests sections, mandates nothing. |
| GitHub Copilot custom-instructions docs | Primary/official for copilot-instructions.md, `applyTo` scoping, 2-page cap, validated-commands dos/don'ts. |
| github.com/josix/awesome-claude-md (cloned, 104 analyses read via subagent) | Curated secondary with an explicit 100-point rubric; analyses are third-party interpretations, not the source files themselves — good for pattern frequency, weaker for verbatim phrasing. |
| github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code (cloned, README + resources CSV) | Curated secondary; its Security/Linting/From-Anthropic categories evidence the deterministic-controls ecosystem; entry descriptions are editorial. |
| DataCamp — "Writing the best CLAUDE.md" | Solid secondary tutorial; consistent with primary docs; several claims (code-fence-verbatim, incident-traceability) are single-source opinion. |
| Braincuber — "Writing best CLAUDE.md complete guide" | **Low independent value: near-verbatim mirror of the DataCamp piece** (identical section list, deletion test, negation advice). Treated as one source, not two. |
| ayautomate — "Best CLAUDE.md examples" | Secondary listicle; useful example inventory and the 100-400-line claim; no determinism coverage; treat length claim as opinion. |
| HumanLayer — "Writing a good CLAUDE.md" | High-quality practitioner essay; instruction-budget argument (~150-200 instructions) and "pointers over copies" are its distinctive, widely cited contributions. |

title: core scaffold: project-overview and fenced commands fragments
description:
The two most-corroborated blocks (every source puts them first) are missing from the core scaffold: a project-overview fragment (purpose in 1-2 lines, stack with versions, entry points, pointer to architecture docs — NOT an embedded directory map, per Anthropic /doctor trim guidance) and a commands fragment whose body is fenced, verbatim-runnable command groups (setup/test/single-test/lint). install should scaffold per-repo overlay stubs for both so every consumer starts with them.

## Design

Content is inherently per-repo, so these land as install-scaffolded overlay stubs (written once when absent, never overwritten — same contract as tasks.json/basicly-gates.yml), created as status: draft so placeholder text never pollutes projections (planner filters to active); the consumer fills them in and flips to active. This repo dogfoods by authoring real, active versions in its own overlay.

## Acceptance Criteria

- basicly install scaffolds .basicly-local/fragments/user/project/project-overview.fragment.yaml and .basicly-local/fragments/user/commands/commands.fragment.yaml when absent, with draft status and a fill-me structure (overview: purpose, stack, entry points, docs pointer; commands: fenced setup/test/single-test/lint groups).
- Existing files at those paths are never overwritten on re-install.
- Scaffolded stubs load as valid fragments (catalog-lint passes with them present) and, being draft, do not change projections until activated.
- Unit tests cover: scaffold-when-absent, no-overwrite, stub validity.
- Dogfood: this repo's overlay gains real active project-overview and commands fragments; projections regenerated; all checks green.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: close the env write deny gap and annotate the confirmation list
description:
settings.json denies Read(.env*) but not Edit/Write — the require-explicit-confirmation prose item about .env edits is deterministically enforceable today. Add Edit(.env*)/Write(.env*) deny rules; then annotate require-explicit-confirmation so items that are hard-denied (force-push, --no-verify, rm -rf, reset --hard, .env writes) read as rationale for the gate, and only genuinely judgment-y items (dependency changes, CI/CD edits, new network calls) stay purely suggestive. User approved the recorded proposal on 2026-07-16.

## Acceptance Criteria

- .claude/settings.json permissions.deny gains Edit and Write rules for the four .env path patterns mirroring the existing Read denies.
- The require-explicit-confirmation fragment states which items the deny-list hard-blocks for Claude Code (prose remains as rationale and for other agents) and keeps the judgment items suggestive; the single IMPORTANT marker is preserved.
- Projections regenerated; catalog-lint, check, and the full test suite green.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: project agent-native tool-time hooks (protect generated files)
description:
Git hooks gate only at commit time; Anthropic's model gates at tool time (PreToolUse, exit 2 blocks) and turn end (Stop). Add a projection of agent-native hooks into .claude/settings.json, starting with a protect-files guard for generated outputs (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, copilot-instructions.md, rules/instructions files, projected skills) — the do-not-edit-generated-files rule currently lives only in prose. Tier-1 curated pattern (Pydantic's DO NOT EDIT blocks).

## Design

- New stdlib-only script .basicly/core/hooks/protect-generated.py: reads the PreToolUse JSON payload from stdin, extracts tool_input.file_path, and exits 2 (block, stderr shown to the model with the edit-the-source alternative) when the target file carries a 'Generated by ... basicly' marker in its first lines or is the generated manifest json; fails open on malformed input or missing files.
- hooks.yaml entries gain an optional manager field (default git); the new hook is manager claude, stage pretooluse. Git-side rendering (pre-commit config, stage installation) filters to manager git.
- claude_settings.py gains merge/check functions that project manager-claude hooks into the hooks.PreToolUse section of the committed .claude/settings.json (matcher Edit|Write|MultiEdit|NotebookEdit), preserving all consumer keys and any foreign hooks; idempotent by managed command match.
- hooks-build applies the settings projection; hooks-check reports drift.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Editing a marker-carrying generated file through Edit/Write is blocked by the hook script (exit 2) with a message naming the catalog source workflow; normal files pass; malformed payloads fail open.
- basicly hooks-build writes the PreToolUse wiring into .claude/settings.json without disturbing existing settings; re-running is a no-op; hooks-check flags a removed or altered managed entry.
- pre-commit config and git stage installation are unchanged by the new manager field (git hooks keep working; agent hooks never reach pre-commit).
- Unit tests cover the script verdicts, the settings merge/check, and the manager filtering.
- Dogfood: this repo's .claude/settings.json gains the wiring via hooks-build; piping a synthetic PreToolUse payload for CLAUDE.md into the script blocks it.

- self-improvement-retro names the quirks category as where approved incident rules land, keeping the one-incident-one-bullet discipline.
- Dogfood: this repo gains an active quirks fragment for the node-resolution trap; projections regenerated; all gates green.
- A regression test covers loading a quirks-category fragment.

title: Add install provenance/state tracking (.basicly/state/install.json)
description:
Record installed catalog version + provenance so upgrades can be reasoned about and `basicly check` can report drift (architecture §9). Written by `basicly install` when it materializes the bundled catalog into a consumer repo (skipped in the authoring repo, where core is its own source). Snapshot the on-disk core state right after materialize, so a later hash mismatch means a hand-edit — the provenance basis the core-sync task basicly-zrj.12.2 needs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `basicly install` in a consumer repo writes `.basicly/state/install.json` (path follows the configured core root) recording: schema version, basicly version, install timestamp, and a per-file sha256 map of the managed core as materialized.
- A second install refreshes the timestamp/version and re-snapshots; the file is valid JSON with a stable schema.
- `basicly check` surfaces provenance drift as advisory notes: core files modified or removed relative to the recorded snapshot, and an installed-vs-current basicly version mismatch. Exit code contract of `check` is unchanged by these notes.
- The authoring repo (core == bundled source) writes no state file and reports no provenance notes.
- Unit/integration tests cover: state written on install, re-install refresh, modified-core drift note, and authoring-repo skip.

title: loop advance from a linked worktree should refuse the merge/ship transition
description:
Field incident shipping basicly-yzyd.1: running 'basicly loop advance' with cwd inside a loop worktree (not the base checkout) made the merge fail ('merge must run from the base checkout ... currently on harness/<name>') yet the ship-checkpoint approval and teardown still proceeded, leaving the child CLOSED but UNMERGED with its commit stranded on the harness branch. The engine should detect it is running from a linked worktree (or that 'main' is not the checked-out base) and refuse the merge/ship transition with a clear error, instead of approving-and-stranding. Also: worktree cleanup --force did not remove the orphaned .git/basicly-worktrees/<name>.json session record once the branch was already deleted, and that stale record counts toward the worktree concurrency cap. Recovery used: git rebase main harness/<name> + git merge --ff-only + git branch -d + rm the stale session json.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Provision a leaf worktree via the loop and cd into it (the linked worktree)
2. From inside the worktree, run 'basicly loop advance <id>' at the build or ship phase
3. Observe the merge fail on base-checkout assertion while ship approval/teardown still closes the bead, stranding the commit on harness/<name>

## Acceptance Criteria

* loop advance refuses the build and ship transitions when run from a linked worktree (git-dir != git-common-dir), returning a graceful blocked result that names the base checkout and mutates no tracker/worktree state
* worktree cleanup drops the session record when the branch is already gone, so no orphaned .json counts toward the concurrency cap
* regression tests cover the linked-worktree refusal and the already-deleted-branch cleanup
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

design: Source: core/skills/<slug>/skill.yaml with a yaml-language-server header. Loader globs */skill.yaml and parses metadata plus the instructions block scalar into SkillDefinition. Format is YAML (not Python) for zero md-discovery collisions and consistency with targets and hooks yaml.

title: loop: feature fan-in merge collides with child self-landing
description:
Found shipping basicly-ajq. loop._build_children fan-in merges every child worktree via merge.merge_queue once all children are closed — but a child driven through its own loop (the flow the harness-loop skill documents: advance lands the worktree, ship tears it down and closes) leaves no worktree behind, so the parent's advance fails with "no worktree session named '<child>'" and the feature can never advance past build. Workaround used: record the parent verify gate directly (basicly verify --mode full --issue <id>), then approve ship and loop run.

Two coherent fixes; pick one and align the harness-loop skill with it:
(a) parent skips children whose branches are already merged (self-landing stays the documented child flow), or
(b) children stop self-landing (their loop ends at 'work committed'; the parent owns the merge queue) and the skill/docs change accordingly.

Chosen: (a) — in loop._build_children, filter merge-queue items to children with a live worktree session (worktree.list_sessions()); self-landed torn-down children count as already landed and are mentioned in the detail message.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Create a feature issue with two child tasks (br create --parent).
2. Drive each child through its own full loop: advance provisions its worktree, build/verify pass, advance lands the worktree, ship tears it down and closes the child.
3. With all children closed and no child worktree sessions remaining, run `basicly loop advance <parent>` to trigger the build fan-in.
4. Observed: SystemExit "no worktree session named '<child>'" from merge.merge_queue; the feature can never advance past build. Expected: fan-in treats the self-landed children as already merged and advances build -> verify.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A feature whose children each landed and closed through their own loop advances build -> verify without manual gate recording
- merge_queue (or its caller) treats an already-merged, torn-down child as satisfied rather than an error
- A regression test covers the children-self-landed path
- The harness-loop skill's phase table matches the implemented flow
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: commit-messages CI gate checks only the subject and misses body-trailer bead ids
description:
## Problem

The quality-gates commit-messages job feeds only the commit SUBJECT (git log %s) to beads-commit-msg.py, but the repo convention puts the beads issue id as a trailing BODY line (and dotted child ids like basicly-onb.6.2 cannot appear in the subject per the conventional-commits rules). So every commit with the id in the body fails CI with 'Missing/unknown beads issue id', even though the local commit-msg hooks (which see the full message) pass.

## Acceptance Criteria

- CI validates the full commit message (subject + body), matching the local commit-msg hooks
- A commit with the beads id in a trailing body line passes the CI commit-messages gate
- The previously-failing commit 5b5f3e9 passes under the fixed check
close_reason: Fixed the commit-messages CI gate to validate the full commit message (git log %B) instead of only the subject (%s), matching the local commit-msg hooks. Verified by simulating the fixed loop over the last 6 commits (all pass, including the previously-failing 5b5f3e9).

title: Catalog selection / flavors (install fragments+hooks but not skills)
description:
§9/§11.8: let a consumer choose a subset of the catalog at install time. Design settled 2026-07-16 (see comment): technology scoping — sources carry an optional `technologies:` list (absent = universal, always ships); install records the repo's selection in `basicly.toml` (default: all); projection commands filter unselected sources and the manifest sweep removes outputs a narrowed selection excludes; core sync stays full. Tag vocabulary is a controlled list enforced by catalog-lint. v2 (out of scope): per-block technology conditioning inside agent slots.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Catalog sources (skills, fragments, agents-fragments, hooks) accept an optional `technologies:` list; a source without it is universal and always projected
- catalog-lint rejects a `technologies:` value outside the controlled vocabulary (stack tags like python/go plus environment tools like zsh/tmux/starship/wezterm)
- `basicly install` accepts a technology-selection flag and records the selection in `basicly.toml`; omitting it selects everything (current behavior unchanged)
- `basicly build` / `skills-build` / `agents-build` / `hooks-build` skip sources whose `technologies:` has no overlap with the recorded selection; core sync still copies the full catalog
- Narrowing the selection then rebuilding sweeps the now-excluded projections via the manifest (no stale outputs left)
- A repo with no selection recorded in `basicly.toml` projects the full catalog (backward compatible)
- Regression tests cover: universal source always ships, tagged source filtered out, selection recorded/read from basicly.toml, sweep after narrowing, lint rejection of unknown tags
- docs/architecture.md documents the property, the selection, and the filtering point
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop
