title: agents as a catalog kind: compose subagent files from building-block fragments
description:
Gap (user, 2026-07-16): basicly projects fragments, skills, and hooks — but not agent files. Subagent definitions (.claude/agents/*.md) are hand-authored per repo with no single source of truth, so they drift and cannot be centrally improved.

Vision: agent files become generated artifacts composed by the projector from an essential-blocks taxonomy (candidate blocks: security, planning, git, orchestration, conventions, documentation, hygiene, hardening, enforcement, verify, validate, authorize, authenticate, context, work tracking, work scoping, review, gates). Core blocks are owned by basicly devs; consumers extend or override specific blocks with custom building blocks via the .basicly-local overlay — the same core/overlay model fragments already use. Generated agent files carry the generated marker, are gated by protect-generated, and must never be edited manually.

Emission is single-source per the 2f4 policy: .claude/agents/ only — VS Code parses the Claude sub-agent format natively (user-shared research finding, 2026-07-16), and Claude Code reads only .claude/.

Prerequisite research: the 84v SOTA research covered instruction-file blocks, not subagent-file anatomy. A dedicated research pass (Anthropic sub-agents spec + VS Code custom-agent docs + curated corpus: awesome-claude-code, VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents, wshobson/agents) must establish the frontmatter contract, the recurring body anatomy, and a frequency-ranked block taxonomy before the schema is designed. Findings land as comments on this bead.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Research findings recorded on this bead: frontmatter contract, cross-tool field compatibility, body anatomy, ranked building-block taxonomy mapped to the candidate list
- New agents catalog kind: YAML sources in .basicly/core (schema + catalog-lint coverage + scaffold command consistent with skills-new/fragment-new)
- Projector composes and renders .claude/agents/*.md with the generated marker; protect-generated blocks manual edits
- Overlay can add whole agents and extend/override individual blocks of core agents
- basicly check/build/install/uninstall handle the new kind (manifest-tracked, stale-file sweep)
- docs/architecture.md documents the kind, the composition model, and the single-source emission policy
- Tests cover schema validation, composition/override precedence, rendering, and the sweep
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

comments.author: niksa
comments.text:
Research 1/2 — Subagent file specification (primary sources, 2026-07-16)

# Subagent Definition Files: Specification and Best-Practices Research

Research date: 2026-07-16. All quotes verbatim from the cited pages as fetched on this date.

---

## A. Frontmatter contract for `.claude/agents/*.md` per Anthropic

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents (primary, official).

Subagent files are Markdown with YAML frontmatter; the body after the frontmatter is the subagent's system prompt. "The frontmatter defines the subagent's metadata and configuration. The body becomes the system prompt that guides the subagent's behavior. Subagents receive only this system prompt plus basic environment details like the working directory, not the full Claude Code system prompt."

### Fields ("Only `name` and `description` are required.")

| Field | Required | Type | Default | Constraints / semantics (per docs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `name` | Yes | string | — | "Unique identifier using lowercase letters and hyphens. Hooks receive this value as `agent_type`. The filename doesn't have to match" |
| `description` | Yes | string | — | "When Claude should delegate to this subagent" — drives automatic delegation (see below) |
| `tools` | No | comma-separated string of tool names | inherit all | "Tools the subagent can use. Inherits all tools if omitted. If no entry in the list resolves to a tool, the subagent fails to launch with an error naming the entries. To preload Skills into context, use the `skills` field rather than listing `Skill` here." Accepts MCP server patterns `mcp__<server>` / `mcp__<server>__*`. `Agent` grants nested spawning; `Agent(type,…)` allowlist syntax applies only when the agent runs as the main thread via `claude --agent` (inside a subagent definition "any type list inside the parentheses is ignored") |
| `disallowedTools` | No | comma-separated string | — | "Tools to deny, removed from inherited or specified list." "If both are set, `disallowedTools` is applied first, then `tools` is resolved against the remaining pool. A tool listed in both is removed." Also accepts `mcp__<server>` and `mcp__*` |
| `model` | No | string | `inherit` | "`sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`, `fable`, a full model ID (for example, `claude-opus-4-8`), or `inherit`. Defaults to `inherit`." Resolution order: `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` env var → per-invocation `model` parameter → frontmatter → main conversation's model; values are checked against the org `availableModels` allowlist |
| `permissionMode` | No | enum | `default` (inherited context) | `default`, `acceptEdits`, `auto`, `dontAsk`, `bypassPermissions`, `plan`, or `manual` (alias for `default`, v2.1.200+). Ignored for plugin subagents. A parent in `bypassPermissions`/`acceptEdits` "takes precedence and can't be overridden"; a parent in auto mode makes frontmatter `permissionMode` ignored |
| `maxTurns` | No | integer | — | "Maximum number of agentic turns before the subagent stops" |
| `skills` | No | YAML list | — | "Skills to preload into the subagent's context at startup. The full skill content is injected, not only the description." Cannot preload skills with `disable-model-invocation: true`; missing/disabled skills are skipped with a debug-log warning |
| `mcpServers` | No | YAML list (string refs or inline server defs) | — | Inline defs use the `.mcp.json` schema keyed by server name; types `stdio`, `http`, `sse`, `ws`. Inline servers connect at subagent start, disconnect at finish; string refs share the parent's connection. Ignored for plugin subagents |
| `hooks` | No | object (hook events) | — | "Lifecycle hooks scoped to this subagent." All hook events supported; frontmatter `Stop` hooks are "automatically converted to `SubagentStop`" when run as a subagent. Ignored for plugin subagents |
| `memory` | No | enum | off | `user` (`~/.claude/agent-memory/<name>/`), `project` (`.claude/agent-memory/<name>/`), or `local` (`.claude/agent-memory-local/<name>/`). Enables Read/Write/Edit automatically and injects the first 200 lines / 25KB of `MEMORY.md`. Docs: "`project` is the recommended default scope" |
| `background` | No | boolean | unset (Claude chooses; background by default since v2.1.198) | "Set to `true` to always run this subagent as a background task, even when Claude needs its result right away" |
| `effort` | No | enum | inherits from session | `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh`, `max`; "available levels depend on the model" |
| `isolation` | No | enum | — | "Set to `worktree` to run the subagent in a temporary git worktree… branched by default from your default branch… automatically cleaned up if the subagent makes no changes" |
| `color` | No | enum | — | `red`, `blue`, `green`, `yellow`, `purple`, `orange`, `pink`, `cyan` — display color in task list/transcript |
| `initialPrompt` | No | string | — | "Auto-submitted as the first user turn when this agent runs as the main session agent (via `--agent` or the `agent` setting)… Prepended to any user-provided prompt" |

The JSON `--agents` CLI flag accepts the same fields plus `prompt` ("Use `prompt` for the system prompt, equivalent to the markdown body in file-based subagents").

Security carve-out: "plugin subagents don't support the `hooks`, `mcpServers`, or `permissionMode` frontmatter fields. These fields are ignored when loading agents from a plugin."

### Locations and precedence

| Location | Scope | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| Managed settings (`.claude/agents/` inside the managed settings directory) | Organization-wide | 1 (highest) |
| `--agents` CLI flag (JSON, session-only) | Current session | 2 |
| `.claude/agents/` | Current project | 3 |
| `~/.claude/agents/` | All your projects | 4 |
| Plugin's `agents/` directory | Where plugin is enabled | 5 (lowest) |

Additional rules: both project and user directories are scanned **recursively**; "The subdirectory path doesn't affect how a subagent is identified or invoked, because identity comes only from the `name` frontmatter field." Project discovery walks up from the cwd; since v2.1.178 the definition closest to the working directory wins for a duplicated name. Duplicates within one directory tree load "chosen by filesystem read order rather than a documented precedence" (flagged by `/doctor`). Plugin subfolders become part of the scoped identifier (`my-plugin:review:security`). Directories added with `--add-dir` are also scanned. Files are hot-reloaded ("Claude Code watches `~/.claude/agents/` and `.claude/agents/`… no restart needed"), except a scope's first-ever `agents` directory created mid-session.

The Claude Code settings page adds a top-level `agent` setting: "Run the main thread as a named subagent… Applies that subagent's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model" (CLI `--agent <name>` overrides it), and `permissions.deny: ["Agent(<name>)"]` disables specific subagents.

### How `description` drives automatic delegation (verbatim)

> "Claude uses each subagent's description to decide when to delegate tasks. When you create a subagent, write a clear description so Claude knows when to use it."

> "Claude automatically delegates tasks based on the task description in your request, the `description` field in subagent configurations, and current context. To encourage proactive delegation, include phrases like 'use proactively' in your subagent's description field."

The official examples use trigger phrasing inside `description`: "Use immediately after writing or modifying code.", "Use proactively when encountering any issues.", "Use proactively for data analysis tasks and queries.", "Use when analyzing data or generating reports."

Note: the `/agents` interactive creation wizard was removed in v2.1.198 — "running it prints a reminder to ask Claude or edit `.claude/agents/` directly. Subagent files, frontmatter fields, and the `.claude/agents/` and `~/.claude/agents/` locations are unchanged." Built-in subagent names (`Explore`, `Plan`, `general-purpose`) can be shadowed by a project/user subagent of the same name.

---

## B. What VS Code parses from the Claude agent format

Source: VS Code custom agents doc (current canonical URL: https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agent-customization/custom-agents; the older https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/customization/custom-agents path still resolves to the same content) and VS Code release notes.

**Yes, `.claude/agents/` is read natively.** VS Code "detects `.md` files in the `.claude/agents` folder" following the Claude sub-agent format. This shipped as part of the "Claude compatibility" initiative in **VS Code 1.109 (January 2026 release, published 2026-02-04)**, which stated: "If you use both VS Code and Claude, you no longer need to maintain separate configuration files." That release also picked up `CLAUDE.md`, `.claude/rules`, `.claude/skills`, and hooks from `.claude/settings.json`. (The requested v1.128 and v1.129 release notes contain nothing on the agent file format: v1.128 covers multi-chat Claude agent sessions and read-only subagent transcript views in the Agents window; v1.129 covers prompt-file→skills migration and the Agent Host Protocol.)

**Claude-format frontmatter fields honored by VS Code** (documented subset):

| Field | Type per VS Code docs | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `name` | string (required) | honored |
| `description` | string | honored |
| `tools` | comma-separated string | honored — "VS Code maps Claude-specific tool names to the corresponding VS Code tools." |
| `disallowedTools` | comma-separated string | honored |
| everything else (`model`, `permissionMode`, `hooks`, `skills`, `mcpServers`, `memory`, `effort`, `isolation`, `color`, `background`, `maxTurns`, `initialPrompt`) | — | not documented as parsed; treat as ignored by VS Code |

The docs note: "Both the VS Code `.agent.md` format (with YAML arrays for tools) and the Claude format (with comma-separated strings) are supported… This enables you to use the same agent definitions across VS Code and Claude Code."

**The native `.agent.md` format** (workspace `.github/agents/`, user profile, or extra folders; introduced in VS Code 1.106, October 2025, when "chat modes" were renamed to custom agents and `.chatmode.md` files became auto-migrated legacy) differs field-by-field:

| `.agent.md` field | Type | Default | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `name` | string | filename | identifier/display name |
| `description` | string | — | shown in chat input placeholder; also used for agent-to-agent delegation |
| `argument-hint` | string | — | no Claude equivalent |
| `tools` | YAML array | all tools | supports `<server>/*` MCP wildcards; different naming from Claude tool names |
| `agents` | YAML array | — | which subagents this agent may invoke; `*` all, `[]` none. Claude's analog is `Agent(...)` inside `tools` |
| `model` | string or array | currently selected model | array = "system selects first available"; Claude's `model` is a single alias/ID/`inherit` |
| `user-invocable` | boolean | `true` | dropdown visibility; no Claude equivalent |
| `disable-model-invocation` | boolean | `false` | opts out of automatic delegation; Claude has this on skills, not agents |
| `target` | string | both | `vscode` or `github-copilot` |
| `mcp-servers` | array | — | cloud-agent oriented; Claude uses `mcpServers` (different spelling and schema) |
| `handoffs` | array | — | subfields `label`, `agent`, `prompt`, `send` (default `false`), `model`; no Claude equivalent |
| `hooks` | object | — | gated behind the `chat.useCustomAgentHooks` setting |

**Settings gating discovery**: `chat.agentFilesLocations` (additional workspace locations), `chat.useCustomizationsInParentRepositories` (monorepo parent-root discovery), `github.copilot.chat.organizationCustomAgents.enabled` (org-level agents), `chat.useCustomAgentHooks` (agent-scoped hooks).

**Body handling difference that matters for a generator**: in Claude Code the body *replaces* the system prompt for the subagent; in VS Code, "Guidelines in the custom agent file body are prepended to the user chat prompt." VS Code bodies may reference tools with `#tool:<tool-name>` syntax and link to other instruction files via Markdown links — both meaningless to Claude Code.

---

## C. Official guidance on writing the body / system prompt

From Anthropic's sub-agents page ("Best practices" tip block, verbatim):

> - "**Design focused subagents:** each subagent should excel at one specific task"
> - "**Write detailed descriptions:** Claude uses the description to decide when to delegate"
> - "**Limit tool access:** grant only necessary permissions for security and focus"
> - "**Check into version control:** share project subagents with your team"

Structural pattern used by every official example (code-reviewer, debugger, data-scientist, db-reader):
1. **Role statement** first line: "You are a senior code reviewer ensuring high standards…", "You are an expert debugger specializing in root cause analysis."
2. **"When invoked:" numbered startup procedure** (e.g., "1. Run git diff to see recent changes / 2. Focus on modified files / 3. Begin review immediately").
3. **Domain checklist / process bullets** (review checklist, debugging process, key practices).
4. **Output contract** ("Provide feedback organized by priority: Critical issues (must fix) / Warnings (should fix) / Suggestions (consider improving)").
5. **Closing constraint** ("Focus on fixing the underlying issue, not the symptoms.", "You cannot modify data. If asked to INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or modify schema, explain that you only have read access.").

Trigger/proactive phrasing lives in `description`, not the body: "To encourage proactive delegation, include phrases like 'use proactively' in your subagent's description field." Official example descriptions pair a capability sentence with a "Use …" trigger sentence ("Expert code review specialist. Proactively reviews code for quality, security, and maintainability. Use immediately after writing or modifying code.").

Other body-relevant facts from the docs:
- The body is the whole system prompt; the subagent does not get the Claude Code system prompt, but (except built-in Explore/Plan) it *does* get CLAUDE.md/memory hierarchy and a git-status snapshot, so rules already in CLAUDE.md need not be duplicated.
- Tool restriction has three tiers: `tools` allowlist, `disallowedTools` denylist, and `PreToolUse` hooks "when you need finer control than the `tools` field provides" (e.g., read-only SQL validation).
- Memory-enabled agents should carry self-maintenance instructions in the body: "Update your agent memory as you discover codepaths, patterns, library locations, and key architectural decisions…"
- Best-practices page: use subagents for high-volume/verbose work, scoped investigation, and adversarial review ("Before treating a task as done, have a subagent review the diff in a fresh context and report gaps"), and warns reviewers should "flag only gaps that affect correctness or the stated requirements."
- GitHub's contract for the body: "Define the agent's behavior, expertise, and instructions in the Markdown content below the YAML frontmatter. The prompt can be a maximum of 30,000 characters." — a hard length ceiling a generator should enforce for cross-tool output.

---

## D. Cross-tool compatibility matrix

Field spellings as each tool documents them. CC = Claude Code (`.claude/agents/*.md`), VSC-C = VS Code reading the Claude format, VSC-A = VS Code `.agent.md`, GH = GitHub.com Copilot cloud agent (`.github/agents/*.md`).

| Field | CC | VSC-C | VSC-A | GH | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `name` | required | required | optional (filename default) | optional (filename default) | CC: lowercase+hyphens; GH filenames limited to `. - _ a-z A-Z 0-9` |
| `description` | required; drives delegation | yes | yes; placeholder + delegation | **required** | The only field required everywhere except CC's `name` |
| `tools` | comma-separated string | yes (mapped tool names) | YAML array, `<server>/*` | list or comma string, wildcards | Tool *names* differ per tool even where the field is shared |
| `disallowedTools` | yes | yes | no | no | Claude-format only |
| `model` | alias / full ID / `inherit` (default `inherit`) | not documented | string or array (first available) | string | Value vocabularies incompatible (Claude aliases vs Copilot model names) |
| `permissionMode` | yes | no | no | no | CC only |
| `maxTurns` | yes | no | no | no | CC only |
| `skills` | yes (preload) | no | no | no | CC only |
| `mcpServers` | yes (list; refs or inline) | no | — | — | CC spelling |
| `mcp-servers` | no | — | listed but "not used in VS Code or IDEs" | yes (cloud agent) | GH spelling |
| `hooks` | yes | no | yes, gated by `chat.useCustomAgentHooks` | no | Schemas unrelated (CC hook events vs VS Code agent hooks) |
| `memory` | yes | no | no | no | CC only |
| `background` / `effort` / `isolation` / `color` / `initialPrompt` | yes | no | no | no | CC only |
| `agents` | no (uses `Agent(...)` in `tools`) | no | yes | not documented for GH | |
| `argument-hint` | no | no | yes | ignored ("currently not supported for Copilot cloud agent on GitHub.com") | |
| `handoffs` | no | no | yes | ignored on GitHub.com | |
| `user-invocable` | no | no | yes (default `true`) | yes (default `true`) | |
| `disable-model-invocation` | no (skill-level concept in CC) | no | yes (default `false`) | yes (default `false`) | GH: "Disables Copilot cloud agent from automatically using this custom agent based on task context" |
| `target` | no | no | yes (`vscode`/`github-copilot`) | yes | |
| `infer` | no | no | retired | retired — "use `disable-model-invocation` and `user-invocable` instead" | |
| `metadata` | no | no | "not used in VS Code or IDEs" | yes | |
| Markdown body | system prompt (replaces default) | system instructions | "prepended to the user chat prompt"; `#tool:` refs | prompt, max 30,000 chars | Semantics differ: replacement vs prepend |

**Portable core for a generator**: `name` + `description` (with a "Use …" trigger sentence) + `tools` + body. Everything else is per-tool. GitHub dedupes across repo/org/enterprise levels by filename "(minus `.md` or `.agent.md`)… so that the lowest level configuration takes precedence."

---

## E. Sources table

| Source | Credibility |
|---|---|
| https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents | Primary/official — Anthropic Claude Code docs; authoritative spec for `.claude/agents` frontmatter |
| https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices | Primary/official — Anthropic; guidance on subagent usage, descriptions, adversarial review |
| https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings | Primary/official — Anthropic; `agent` setting, `disableAgentView`, `permissions.deny Agent(...)` |
| https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/agent-customization/custom-agents (also served at /docs/copilot/customization/custom-agents) | Primary/official — Microsoft VS Code docs; `.agent.md` spec and Claude-format support statement |
| https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_109 | Primary/official — VS Code release notes; dates the native `.claude/agents` "Claude compatibility" to Jan 2026 release |
| https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_106 (via search excerpt + microsoft/vscode-docs mirror) | Primary/official — dates `.github/agents` / `.agent.md` introduction and chat-mode rename to Oct 2025 |
| https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_128, /v1_129 | Primary/official — checked as requested; contain no agent-file-format changes (UI/session features only) |
| https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/reference/custom-agents-configuration | Primary/official — GitHub Docs; authoritative `.github/agents` property reference incl. retired `infer`, 30k-char body limit |
| https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/concepts/agents/cloud-agent/about-custom-agents | Primary/official — GitHub Docs; repo/org/enterprise locations and supported surfaces |
| visualstudiomagazine.com articles (VS Code 1.109/1.121/1.128 Claude coverage) | Secondary — trade press; used only to locate the correct release notes, not as a spec source |

**Caveats**: the VS Code docs do not state whether `model` in a Claude-format file is honored (only `name`/`description`/`tools`/`disallowedTools` are listed), and they do not version-stamp the Claude-format feature inside the doc page itself — the v1.109 release notes are the dating evidence. GitHub's reference page does not document level precedence beyond the filename-deduplication rule quoted above.

comments.author: niksa
comments.text:
Research 2/2 — Community subagent corpus survey (2026-07-16)

# Community Claude Code Subagent Files: Anatomy, Building Blocks, and Quality Survey

**Corpus**: VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents — 154 agent files in 10 category dirs, median 287 lines, highly templated. wshobson/agents — 203 agent files under plugins/*/agents/, median 156 lines, bimodal (~30-line focused agents and ~150-350-line capability dumps). hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code — current curated CSV has no dedicated subagents category; linked exemplars fetched from dshakes/compass (5 files). Stats computed over the 357 frontmattered files; 32 files read in depth across code review, security audit, pen-test, planning/architecture, orchestration/team-lead, documentation, tutorial, test, debugging, incident response, policy/governance, context management, C4 modeling, git workflow, prompt engineering, SRE.

---

## A. Recurring body anatomy

1. **Role/identity statement — ~95%** (339/357 open with "You are …"). Always first body line, seniority-inflected. Best files fuse identity with an epistemic stance, not a title: "**Default to doubt:** assume the change is broken until the checks prove otherwise. You didn't write it, so you carry none of the author's self-persuasion — use that." (compass code-reviewer)

2. **Purpose / mission expansion — ~33%** (`## Purpose` / `## Core Mission`), a 2-4 sentence elaboration; wshobson signature (59% of its files).

3. **Activation steps ("When invoked: 1…n") — 37% overall** (84% of Volt, 2% of wshobson). E.g. "1. Capture error message and stack trace / 2. Identify reproduction steps / 3. Isolate the failure location / 4. Implement minimal fix / 5. Verify solution works" (wshobson debugger).

4. **Domain capability inventory — ~85%+** carry topical bullet inventories. Good form: each bullet is an analysis move ("Error signature analysis: exception types, message patterns, frequency, first occurrence"). Bad form: aspirational encyclopedia ("Quantum-safe cryptography… Homomorphic encryption…" in a code security-auditor).

5. **Process / numbered steps — ~87%** — the most universal block after the role line. Best: "## Method (scientific, not shotgun) 1. **Reproduce.** … If you can't reproduce it, say so — don't guess-fix. … 4. **Prove it.** … Iterate until proven, not until it 'seems fixed.'" (compass debugger). Incident archetype adds timeboxing ("Immediate Actions (First 5 minutes)").

6. **Checklists — 42%** (85% of Volt, 9% of wshobson). Two species: verification checklists of checkable facts (good — "metadata.json is valid JSON") and fake-metric checklists (bad — see anti-patterns).

7. **Output format contract — ~22% overall but present in essentially ALL best-in-class files.** The clearest quality discriminator in the corpus. Styles: severity-grouped findings ("Group findings by severity: **Blocking** / **Should-fix** / **Nit**. For each: `path:line — what's wrong — concrete fix`. … If the diff is clean, say so in one line and stop. Don't pad." — compass), fenced literal sample outputs (compass test-runner's PASS/FAIL block), and per-finding schemas shared across parallel reviewers so results merge (wshobson team-reviewer: Location/Dimension/Evidence/Impact/Recommended Fix).

8. **Constraints / boundaries / never-do — ~10% explicit, universal in the best files.** "Never edit files. Never run anything that mutates state (no pushes, no migrations)." "NO architectural decisions — that is the architect's job." "Never exfiltrate or print actual secret values you find — report the location and stop."

9. **Tool discipline (mapping tools to steps) — ~10%, high-value where present.** "1. **Use Glob** to find all relevant files … 4. **Use Bash** only for directory existence checks" (conductor-validator); concrete first command beats tool names ("Get the diff first: `git diff HEAD`").

10. **Behavioral traits — 32%** (wshobson signature at 56%); mostly filler, occasionally operational ("Reports 'no findings' dimensions honestly rather than inflating results").

11. **Example interactions / few-shot — 34-50%.** Strongest files embed example *outputs or artifacts* (full fenced Cedar policies graded by risk profile — policy-enforcer), not quoted user requests.

12. **Inter-agent wiring — Volt-only anti-pattern** (86%): JSON "Communication Protocol" handshake with a fictional context-manager. Contrast wshobson agent-teams which wires through real tools ("Never send structured JSON status messages — use TaskUpdate instead").

13. **Workflow position / handoff contract — ~12 files** (pipeline plugins): declared Input / Output artifact / pipeline position (c4-context, ship-mate, conductor).

14. **When-to-use triggers in the body — ~5%**, duplicating what belongs in `description`.

15. **Embedded artifact templates — ~10%**: fill-in markdown/Mermaid/report skeletons as part of the body.

16. **Closing one-line mission — 47%** ("Focus on risk mitigation. Never break existing functionality without migration path.").

17. **Severity/priority taxonomy** — reviewer/auditor/validator standard: Blocking/Should-fix/Nit; Critical/High/Medium/Low; CRITICAL/WARNING/INFO; P0-P3 SEV tables.

---

## B. Frequency-ranked taxonomy of reusable building blocks

Candidate-list support (357 files):

| Block | Support | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| validate | Strong (56%) | pure validation agents (conductor-validator: categories, severity levels, report template); "untrusted input validated/typed at the boundary" |
| review | Strong | the most convergent archetype: diff-first, severity grouping, path:line citations; 8+ code-reviewer variants |
| verify | Strong (40%) | "Run them and read the real output"; "Prove it. Make the smallest change or probe that confirms or kills the hypothesis" |
| security | Strong (38%) | whole archetype family; appears BOTH as subject matter and as operating rules ("Never exfiltrate secrets") — a generator should separate the two |
| orchestration | Strong (36%) | team-lead lifecycle (Spawn→Assign→Monitor→Collect→Synthesize→Shutdown→Cleanup), file-ownership rules, dependency graphs |
| context | Strong-but-skewed (33%) | 84% of Volt is a fictional handshake; the GOOD form is context priming: "Read the relevant code, the project CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md, and any ADRs. Understand the invariants before proposing anything" |
| work scoping | Moderate-strong (31%) | "Scope clearly defined and authorized"; "Out-of-scope boundaries (what this task explicitly does NOT do)"; "Audit the diff or component the caller names" |
| planning | Moderate-strong (24%) | compass architect output contract: Approach / Files to touch / Sequence / Risks & tradeoffs / Verification |
| authorize | Moderate (23%) | two forms: RBAC/ABAC subject matter, and agent-level authorization (Cedar policies gating agent tool calls: "The gate is on write / post / merge / close actions only") |
| gates | Moderate (22%) | quality-gate enforcement bullets; governance plugins gating PR actions behind human approval |
| enforcement | Moderate (21%) | mostly overlaps gates+authorize |
| conventions | Moderate (19%) | "does it match this repo's CLAUDE.md and existing style?" — usually a checklist line inside review agents |
| authenticate | Subject-matter only (19%) | OAuth2/OIDC bullets in security agents; ZERO examples of agents authenticating themselves — treat as a security-domain content fragment, not an operating block |
| documentation | Strong as archetype | docs-architect (quantified output characteristics), tutorial-engineer, c4-* family with embedded templates |
| git | Weak-moderate (6%) | 1-2 dedicated agents; mostly implicit ("git diff HEAD" discipline) |
| hardening | Weak (8%) | folded into security everywhere |
| hygiene | Weak (7%) | best form: "add a temporary log/print if needed — and remove it after"; rarely a named block |
| work tracking | Weak (2%) | only built-in Task tools (agent-teams) and conductor's tracks.md; NO external-tracker integration anywhere in the corpus |

**Blocks the corpus shows that are MISSING from the candidate list**: (1) role/persona identity (universal, 95%); (2) output contract / report format (highest-leverage block in the corpus); (3) severity/priority taxonomy; (4) read-only / no-mutation tool discipline; (5) escalation & clarification protocol ("If you can't reproduce it, say so — don't guess-fix"; "Ask questions one at a time"); (6) handoff / workflow position (declared inputs/outputs/pipeline position); (7) model/cost tiering (haiku for mechanical — "You are cheap and fast on purpose" — opus for judgment); (8) evidence discipline (file:line for every finding; "State explicitly what you did *not* have visibility into"); (9) minimal-change / rollback safety; (10) urgency/phase timeboxing (incident archetype); (11) embedded artifact templates; (12) honesty/anti-inflation clause ("Reports 'no findings' honestly"; "Don't pad").

**Candidate items with no real corpus support**: authenticate (as operating block), work tracking against an external tracker, hygiene as a named block.

---

## C. Frontmatter usage statistics

| Field | Count | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| name | 100% | kebab-case; wshobson prefixes plugin name |
| description | 100% | median ~238 chars, max 446 |
| model | 98% | sonnet 174, inherit 77, opus 55, haiku 43; compass pins exact IDs |
| tools | 48% | Volt 100%, wshobson 8%; everyone else inherits all tools |
| color | 2.5% | only team/conductor plugins |

No other fields appear in the corpus.

**Description phrasing**: "Use PROACTIVELY …" 44%; "Masters …" 29%; "Use this agent when you need to …" 19%; "Expert …" opener 19%; "Use IMMEDIATELY for production incidents" (urgency variant); "MUST BE USED" — 0 occurrences (folklore from older collections). Best-practice pattern (compass): what it does + trigger conditions + what it returns + permission posture in one description ("…Use proactively after writing a non-trivial chunk of code, before committing… Returns prioritized, actionable findings — not a rewrite. … Read-only.").

**Tools allowlists by archetype**: read-only reviewers/auditors/validators `Read, Grep, Glob` (+Bash for running checks); research `+ WebFetch, WebSearch`; code writers `Bash, Edit, Glob, Grep, Read, Write` (modal set, 102/170); documentation adds web tools; orchestrators add Agent/Task/messaging tools; debugger keeps Edit but not Write (fix-in-place, no new files).

---

## D. What separates the best files from mediocre

Best-in-class exemplars: dshakes/compass code-reviewer/debugger/security-auditor/architect/test-runner (27-35 lines each); wshobson conductor-validator, agent-teams team-lead + team-reviewer, error-detective (31 lines), legacy-modernizer (35 lines), policy-enforcer, ship-mate orchestrate, docs-architect.

What the best share:
- Single responsibility with declared non-goals ("You review changes; you do not rewrite them")
- An output contract, often with a literal sample
- Concreteness: commands, file names, path:line
- Negative constraints and permission posture stated in the body AND mirrored in frontmatter tools
- Checklists of checkable facts in priority order, not aspirational metrics
- Escalation honesty: what to do when blocked, unreproducible, or clean ("say so in one line and stop")
- Length: ~30-100 lines beats 250-350. Sub-40-line files read as executable procedures; nearly all 280+ line files are reference encyclopedias with no procedure. Exception: 200+ lines justified when the bulk is a fill-in template or example artifacts.
- Cost awareness: model tier matched to judgment vs mechanical work; description tells the router what the agent returns

Mediocre files are domain glossaries: 8-10 Capabilities subsections of noun-phrase bullets, a generic 10-step Response Approach that fits any agent, no output contract, no constraints.

---

## E. Anti-patterns observed

1. **Fabricated-metric checklists**: "Agent selection accuracy > 95% achieved", "Deadlock prevention 100% ensured" — unmeasurable KPIs stated as completed facts; trains the agent to assert success.
2. **Fake telemetry and victory laps**: canned "Delivery notification" strings ("Orchestrated 87 agents processing 234K messages/minute… zero deadlocks") — a script for hallucinated results.
3. **Dependency on a fictional collaborator**: 84% of Volt begins "Query context manager for…" with a JSON handshake to a tool that doesn't exist; invites invented tool calls.
4. **Capability keyword-stuffing**: 100+ noun-phrase bullets spanning quantum cryptography to org change management in one agent.
5. **One template stamped 154 times**: identical skeletons, differentiation only in nouns — the cautionary tale for a fragment generator: composition must not flatten distinct archetypes into one shape.
6. **Description duplicated as body**, including router-addressed "Use PROACTIVELY for…" text aimed at the agent itself.
7. **Tools/model mismatched to the job**: write tools on pure coordinators; haiku on a 275-line judgment agent; 52% omit tools entirely, silently inheriting everything.
8. **Near-duplicate agents across plugins** (same 309-line file in 4+ plugins) — the drift hazard fragment-composition should solve.
9. **Aspirational currency claims**: "2024/2025 best practices", named third-party tools the agent cannot access — stale on arrival.
10. **No output contract on report-producing agents** — makes downstream merging impossible.
11. **Seniority inflation as substance**: "elite/master/25 years of experience" carries no behavior; encode the stance ("default to doubt", "prove it, don't guess-fix") instead of the résumé.
12. **Checklist-as-boast vs checklist-as-procedure**: "Documentation complete thoroughly" vs "metadata.json is valid JSON" — only the latter is executable.

comments.author: niksa
comments.text:
Synthesis 3/3 — Design inputs for the agents catalog kind (2026-07-16)

Combines research 1/2 (primary-source spec) and 2/2 (357-file corpus survey) into decisions the schema design should start from.

## 1. Emission target and portable contract

- Emit to `.claude/agents/*.md` only (single-source, consistent with basicly-2f4). Claude Code treats the body as the full system prompt; VS Code has parsed the format natively since 1.109.
- Frontmatter to emit by default — the portable core only: `name` (kebab, required), `description` (required; drives delegation in both readers), `tools` (comma-separated string), plus `model` (Claude-only but harmless elsewhere). VS Code honors exactly name/description/tools/disallowedTools from the Claude format; everything else in Anthropic's 16-field contract (permissionMode, skills, memory, hooks, maxTurns, effort, isolation, mcpServers, background, color, initialPrompt) is Claude-only — the schema may expose a `claude:` extension map for them, emitted verbatim, defaulted empty.
- Enforce at lint time: body ≤ 30,000 chars (GitHub cloud-agent hard ceiling; cheap portability insurance), name lowercase+hyphens, description non-empty.

## 2. Description composition rule (schema-level, not free text)

Corpus best practice + official docs converge on a 4-part description: (a) what it does, (b) trigger conditions ("Use proactively when/after …" — 44% corpus adoption, officially endorsed; "MUST BE USED" is folklore, 0 corpus occurrences), (c) what it returns, (d) permission posture ("Read-only."). Schema: separate YAML fields (purpose, triggers, returns, posture) that the projector joins — guarantees no part is forgotten and keeps router-addressed text out of the body.

## 3. Body composition skeleton (the slot model)

Five ordered slots, validated independently by Anthropic's official examples and the corpus best-in-class:

1. **role** — identity + epistemic stance (encode the stance, not the résumé: "default to doubt", not "elite 25-year veteran")
2. **startup** — "When invoked:" numbered procedure, first step a concrete command (e.g. `git diff HEAD`)
3. **process** — domain method/checklist; checkable facts in priority order; never aspirational metrics
4. **output-contract** — deliverable shape, ideally with a literal fenced sample; severity taxonomy where applicable; include the "if clean, say so in one line and stop" clause
5. **constraints** — never-do list with alternatives, permission posture restated, escalation honesty ("if you can't reproduce it, say so — don't guess-fix")

Blocks are fragments filling slots; an agent source = archetype + slot fills + overrides. Core blocks basicly-owned; overlay adds agents and overrides individual slots/blocks (same precedence model as fragments).

## 4. Revised block taxonomy (candidate list, corrected by evidence)

- KEEP, strong corpus support: review, validate, verify, security (split into: security-conduct operating rules vs security-domain content), orchestration, context-priming, work-scoping, planning, documentation.
- KEEP, moderate: gates, enforcement, authorize, conventions.
- RECLASSIFY: authenticate → content fragment inside security-domain, not an operating block (zero corpus evidence as behavior). hygiene → single constraint lines (e.g. "remove temporary instrumentation"), fold into constraints slot. hardening → fold into security-domain.
- KEEP DESPITE weak corpus support: work-tracking and git — the corpus has no external-tracker integration at all (2%), but br/harness-loop integration is exactly basicly's differentiator; corpus absence here is a gap in the ecosystem, not evidence against. These blocks are where composed agents reference `br`, `basicly loop`, and the commit discipline.
- ADD (corpus-demanded, missing from the candidate list): role-identity, output-contract, severity-taxonomy, tool-discipline (incl. read-only posture), escalation-clarification, handoff/workflow-position (declared inputs/outputs/pipeline position), evidence-discipline (path:line citations; state what you did NOT see), minimal-change/rollback, honesty/anti-inflation, artifact-templates, model-cost-tiering (schema field, not prose).

## 5. Consistency rules the projector/lint must enforce

- tools ↔ posture coherence: an agent whose constraints say read-only must not be granted Edit/Write (corpus anti-pattern 7: write tools on pure coordinators; 52% omit tools and silently inherit everything — basicly agents always emit an explicit allowlist).
- Archetype tool presets: reviewer/auditor/validator = Read, Grep, Glob (+Bash for checks); researcher adds WebFetch/WebSearch; writer = Bash, Edit, Glob, Grep, Read, Write; orchestrator adds Agent/Task tools; debugger gets Edit but not Write.
- model tier ↔ archetype: mechanical/report agents → cheap tier; judgment agents (review, architecture) → top tier.
- No CLAUDE.md duplication: subagents inherit the project memory hierarchy, so composed bodies must not restate projected fragment rules — reference them ("follow the repo's commit discipline") instead. Deletion-test lint from catalog-authoring applies.
- Length budget: warn > ~100 body lines unless the overage is a fenced artifact template or literal sample output (the two justified exceptions found).
- Anti-pattern lint (reject at catalog-lint): fabricated-metric checklist items ("> 95% achieved"), fake telemetry/victory-lap strings, references to fictional collaborators/tools, description text duplicated in body, aspirational currency claims ("2025 best practices", named third-party tools the agent can't run).
- Anti-flattening: archetypes must keep distinct skeletons (slot model allows per-archetype slot sets); the 154-identical-templates corpus is the cautionary tale.

## 6. Open design questions for the implementation session

1. Archetype set for v1: reviewer, security-auditor, debugger, test-runner, planner/architect, docs-writer, orchestrator? (Pick 3-4 to start; compass files are the quality bar.)
2. Schema shape: one agent.yaml per agent referencing shared block YAMLs, vs blocks inline with `extends:`? (Mirror the fragment/skill loader patterns where possible.)
3. Does the overlay override at block granularity, slot granularity, or whole-agent? (Recommend: whole-agent add + per-slot override; per-block within slot only if cheap.)
4. Should projected agents also get the `claude:` extension fields (memory, permissionMode) in core defaults, or leave those entirely to overlays?

title: Inference layer: capability probe beyond PATH presence
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 4. Runner selection is shutil.which (PATH presence) only; a binary whose headless flag (-p, exec) was dropped or renamed would still be auto-selected and then fail at dispatch. Probe the binary to confirm the assumed headless flag before auto-selecting, and surface it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- runner.probe_capability(spec, *, run=None) -> Capability{reachable, flag_ok, detail}: for a headless runner it runs the binary with --help and checks the static headless-flag tokens (command minus the binary and the {prompt}/{model} placeholders) appear in the output. flag_ok is False ONLY on positive evidence (the probe ran and a flag token is absent); a probe that cannot run assumes capable, so a flaky or slow probe never false-skips a working agent. A handoff runner is trivially capable.
- runner.is_capable(spec, *, which, run) -> bool: on PATH (is_available) and flag_ok.
- select_runner gains an optional capable predicate: auto skips a runner that is on PATH but probes as not capable, falling through to the next candidate and finally the manual handoff. Explicit selection is honored unchanged. With no predicate the current PATH-only behavior is preserved.
- The loop dispatch and the CLI runner resolution pass the real probe so auto confirms the headless flag before selecting.
- runner list surfaces each runner capability (PATH + probe result / detail).
- Regression tests: probe flag-present / flag-absent / unprobeable; headless-flag extraction; is_capable; select_runner skipping an incapable runner and preserving behavior without a predicate; runner list output.
- Architecture section 12.8 updated. pytest, ruff, and projection checks stay green.

## Scope

A --help flag-presence probe only: no --version parsing, no semantic per-flag validation, no basicly status integration (runner list is the surface). Builds on the runner seam (basicly-45ld model field, basicly-140a attribution).

title: Raise the claude+copilot always-on size-warning cap to 9000 (calibrated, not trim)
description:
Decision (2026-07-17): do NOT trim further. The c5to audit already collapsed the redundant verify guidance (6->4 bullets, every distinct rule kept) and the always-on fragments are now dense with no remaining duplication. Trimming ~150 more chars would cost real guidance for a round-number threshold. Instead, recalibrate the soft size-warning cap.

The 8000 cap is a non-blocking warning on 'basicly build' (not a gate, not checked by 'check'/CI), meant to catch always-on context growing enough to dilute attention. Codex already runs at 12000 with a documented rationale. Empirically these files are ~160 lines at ~50 chars/line; ~200 lines (~10000 chars) is the point where attention starts to dilute. A soft warning should fire before that point, not at it.

Fix: raise the claude and copilot targets' max_size_warning from 8000 to 9000 (with a rationale comment), leaving codex at 12000. 9000 clears the current files (CLAUDE.md 8012, copilot 8144) by ~850 chars / ~17 lines while still warning ~1000 chars before the ~10000 danger zone. The cap is warning-only, so no projected content or manifest changes.

## Acceptance Criteria

- claude.yaml and copilot.yaml max_size_warning = 9000 with a rationale comment; codex unchanged at 12000.
- 'basicly build' no longer warns for .claude/CLAUDE.md or .github/copilot-instructions.md.
- 'basicly check', catalog verify, agents-check, and markdownlint stay green; no projected file content changes.
acceptance_criteria: claude.yaml and copilot.yaml max_size_warning=9000 with rationale comment (codex stays 12000); basicly build no longer warns for CLAUDE.md/copilot-instructions.md; check/catalog-verify/agents-check/markdownlint green; no projected content or manifest change.

title: Frictionless tracker for the harness loop - br redirect worktrees and engine-owned tracker commits
description:
Design conclusion from the friction investigation (2026-07-17). Only .beads/issues.jsonl churns; config.yaml/metadata.json are static. br natively supports a git-ignored .beads/redirect file: a worktree redirected at base .beads shares ONE DB+JSONL (verified: redirect wins over checked-out tracked files, no local db created, worktree mutations write base jsonl). Design: (1) worktree provisioning writes .beads/redirect to the base checkout (keep the jsonl copy as fallback for br without redirect support); (2) beads-commit-msg hook becomes redirect-aware so fresh ids resolve live; (3) the ENGINE owns tracker commits - merge_worktree auto-commits tracker-only dirt in base before landing (one rollup commit: claim/classify/binding/gates), and ship auto-commits the close after br close - agents make ZERO manual tracker commits; a dirty base with non-beads changes still blocks; (4) relax the verify linked-worktree guard when the worktree redirects to the main checkout (recording is then safe); (5) update harness-loop/tool-br skills and architecture docs - the pre-provisioning tracker commit disappears entirely. Combined with the CI paths-ignore (basicly-flp), tracker friction drops to zero: create/update/close just work from anywhere, no manual chore commits, no conflicts (the harness branch never modifies jsonl; base is the only writer).

## Acceptance Criteria

- A loop-provisioned worktree gets a .beads/redirect to base and br commands run there hit the base tracker (test). The beads-commit-msg hook accepts a fresh id from a redirected worktree (test). merge_worktree auto-commits tracker-only dirt with a conventional message and still refuses mixed dirt (test). Ship makes the closing tracker commit itself (test). verify --issue works from a redirected worktree and still refuses from a non-redirected one (test). Skills and architecture docs describe the new zero-touch discipline. Full gate suite passes.

title: loop worktree misses freshly-filed tracker state; first commit rejected
description:
Hit twice in one day (basicly-em9 in the authoring repo, term-m3a in the terminal consumer): when an issue is filed and immediately driven through the loop, the worktree branches from the last COMMIT, which predates the issue's .beads/issues.jsonl line — so the first commit in the worktree is rejected by beads-commit-msg (unknown id). Manual workaround each time: commit the filing in the base checkout, then `git checkout main -- .beads/issues.jsonl` inside the worktree.

1. br create <issue> (jsonl dirty, uncommitted).
2. basicly loop advance ... through classify: worktree provisioned from HEAD.
3. Commit work in the worktree referencing the new id: beads-commit-msg rejects — the worktree's issues.jsonl lacks the id.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Loop worktree provisioning ensures the tracked issue's id is known inside the worktree: either the engine commits/requires a clean tracker state before branching (clear error naming the fix), or provisioning syncs .beads/issues.jsonl from the base checkout into the worktree.

title: conventional-commits skill omits the description charset rule the commit-msg hook enforces
description:
The commit-msg hook (.basicly/core/hooks/commit-msg.py) rejects any commit whose description contains characters outside [a-z0-9 space hyphen] — no capitals, no periods/dots. This is a stricter-than-spec house profile: Conventional Commits v1.0.0 itself places NO charset restriction on the description and its body is free-form (verified against the spec). The conventional-commits skill already documents lowercase-only and 'reword version strings', but it tells agents to REWORD out dotted/uppercase forms rather than move them to the free-form body, and it lacks the two real-rejection examples. The hook's error is also generic (does not name the offending character). Two real rejections in one session: 'Font Awesome v4' (capitals) and 'restamp the install to 0.2.0' (the dots in a version number).

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Attempt a commit with a capitalized proper noun (e.g. 'feat: add Font Awesome v4 icons') or a dotted version (e.g. 'chore: restamp the install to 0.2.0').
2. The commit-msg hook rejects it with a generic error that does not point at the offending char.
3. The skill offers no guidance that the exact version/proper-noun form can live in the commit body (which the hook does not validate).

## Acceptance Criteria

- conventional-commits/skill.yaml explicitly states the description charset (lowercase letters, digits, spaces, hyphens only) AND that exact version numbers/dots and proper-noun capitalization belong in the free-form commit body, not the subject; includes the Font Awesome and version-number examples.
- Guidance stays honest that this is stricter than the CC v1.0.0 spec (spec puts no charset limit on the description; body is free-form).
- commit-msg.py error output names the specific disallowed character(s) in the description.
- Catalog rebuilt; projected skill verified in sync (skills-check); commit-msg hook tests cover the char-naming; full suite green.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: DoR: accept the structured br acceptance-criteria field instead of forcing a body heading
description:
Surfaced while shipping basicly-shgo (2026-07-18): br has a structured 'acceptance_criteria' field (settable via 'br update --acceptance-criteria', queryable via 'br show --json'), but basicly's policy.definition_of_ready is a thin wrapper over 'br lint --json' which checks the description BODY for template sections (the '## Acceptance Criteria' heading), not the structured field. Consequence: to pass the decompose Definition-of-Ready gate, acceptance criteria must be duplicated into the body even after being set in the field. Proven: field-only was blocked by DoR; adding the body heading unblocked it.

Design is open for the loop to settle. Note 'br lint' checks MORE than AC (e.g. Steps to Reproduce for bugs), so this is not a clean swap of 'br lint' for a field read - a likely direction is to treat a non-empty structured 'acceptance_criteria' field as SATISFYING the AC template requirement (so authors may use the queryable field OR the body heading) while preserving the other template-section checks. Investigate whether beads_rust's lint template can itself read the field before adding a basicly-side workaround (per the investigate-tool-before-redesign lesson).

title: Tracker-only commits trigger pointless builds - investigate and fix commit discipline
description:
Every push to main triggers CI (and in consumer repos like dreidesk, a long verify build), but the harness loop currently produces multiple .beads/issues.jsonl-only commits per track (claim, loop-state, close after teardown). Investigate WHY tracker commits were separated from work commits (suspected: worktree provisioning syncs base jsonl and committing on both sides makes the landing rebase conflict - see beads-tracker-vs-loop-worktrees memory and basicly-djt). Then judge and decide the optimal fix, e.g.: fold the close into the last work commit (close bead before the resolving commit), batch pushes until the closing commit lands, and/or paths-ignore .beads/** in CI workflows (authoring + the consumer CI template). br is the essential tracker the harness builds on, so the chosen discipline must be conflict-free with worktrees and documented.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Root cause for the separated tracker commits is documented on this issue with evidence from code/history. A decision comment records the chosen design and rejected alternatives. Implementation lands: workflow/CI changes (including the consumer CI template if applicable) and/or loop commit-discipline changes so tracker-only pushes stop triggering full builds and tracker state stays conflict-free with worktree landings. Agent instructions/skills (tool-br, harness-loop, fragments) updated to the new discipline. Full gate suite passes.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: INVESTIGATION (root cause, with evidence): tracker commits are separated from work commits structurally, not accidentally. (1) worktree provisioning copies the BASE WORKING-TREE issues.jsonl into the new worktree (worktree.py create, basicly-djt fix 32dc562) purely so the beads-commit-msg hook recognizes freshly filed ids; (2) merge.py _assert_base_ready requires a CLEAN base checkout at landing, so any base-side tracker mutation (claim, work type, checkpoint approvals - all recorded via br from base) must be committed before the landing advance; (3) the br daemon rewrites the full jsonl export in every checkout, so a harness branch that also commits issues.jsonl guarantees a rebase conflict at merge_worktree step 1 - br sync --merge (reconcile_beads) only runs AFTER a successful merge and cannot rescue the rebase; (4) the close cannot ride the last work commit because loop._on_ship closes the issue AFTER the merge commit exists (phase order: land at build->verify, then verify gate on base, then ship checkpoint, then close). Folding the close into a work commit is therefore impossible in the current two-checkout model; the tool-br rule (close folded into the resolving commit) applies only to non-loop base-checkout work.

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: DECISION: the pointless builds are a CI-trigger problem, not a commit-count problem - GitHub runs the workflows per push to main regardless of content, and the expensive job is the 3-OS quality-gates matrix (and in consumer repos like dreidesk, the scaffolded basicly-gates workflow / their own verify build). Chosen fix: (a) add paths-ignore .beads/** to the push and pull_request triggers of basicly.yml and quality-gates.yml, and to the CONSUMER_CI_WORKFLOW template so new consumer installs get it; (b) keep tracker commits separate (structural, see investigation) but cut them to exactly two per track via skill discipline: ONE pre-provisioning chore(beads) commit folding claim+work-type+classify approval, and ONE closing commit after ship; (c) push discipline: push once per track after the closing commit, never push tracker-only commits alone. Trade-off accepted: a tracker-only push skips CI commit-message validation for those commits; the local commit-msg hooks are the deterministic floor and already validated them. REJECTED (for now): engine redesign folding the close into the merge commit (git merge --no-commit + stage jsonl) - it requires reordering the loop so landing happens AFTER verify+ship approval (today code lands on main BEFORE the ship checkpoint), would also remove the duplicate base re-verify, but changes governance and feature fan-in semantics; filed separately as a design bead. NOTE for existing consumers: install never overwrites the scaffolded CI workflow, so dreidesk must add paths-ignore .beads/** to its own workflow manually.

title: Observability: fleet rollup across housed repos
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 3. `basicly status --json` is single-repo and self-labelled (STATUS_SCHEMA_VERSION) "for fleet loops", and every dispatch drops a run-record into `.basicly/usage/`, but basicly does no aggregation across the housed repos of the dev workspace.

Add a read-only fleet rollup: `basicly status --fleet [--root PATH]` discovers the basicly-installed repos under the workspace root and rolls up, per repo, the `status --json` snapshot plus a run-record summary into one JSON payload. JSON-first, read-only, exit 0.

Rollup model (decided): in-process — call the current engine's `_status_report(repo_root)` on each discovered repo plus `run_record.load_run_records`; a per-repo failure is captured as an `error` field, never failing the whole rollup. Default root is the parent of the current repo; `--root` overrides. Reuses the existing single-repo status + run-record loaders (no new snapshot logic).

## Acceptance Criteria

- `basicly status --fleet` discovers the basicly repos (immediate subdirs of the workspace root containing a `.basicly/` dir), and emits a JSON payload `{schema_version, workspace_root, repos: [...], totals: {...}}`, sorted deterministically by repo name, exit 0.
- Each repo entry carries its name, the `status --json` payload (or an `error` string if the snapshot raises), and a run-record summary (total runs, counts by outcome, distinct agents/models, beads with runs).
- The command writes nothing anywhere (read-only) and returns 0 even when a repo is unreadable, has no `.basicly/`, or has no run-records.
- `--root PATH` overrides the discovered workspace root; the default is the parent of the current repo.
- Totals aggregate the per-repo run-record counts across the fleet.
- Full suite, ruff, ruff format, pyright, bandit, and the projection gates (basicly check / skills-check / agents-check / hooks-check) pass; verified by exercising the real command against this workspace (not just unit tests).

## Notes

- Cross-repo scope is READ-ONLY aggregation only (dimension 3's explicit ask); it respects the workspace boundary (no writes, no cross-repo mutation, no --add-dir).
- Human-formatted fleet table and a subprocess-per-repo execution model are out of scope (JSON-first, in-process).

comments.author: niksa
comments.text:
Design proposal (2026-07-16, needs approval before build):

COMMAND SPLIT (from cli.py inventory)
- Consumer (run in a consumer repo, usually via the pinned uvx or the scaffolded VS Code tasks): install, uninstall, build, check, hooks-build, hooks-check, skills-build, skills-check.
- Contributor (author the catalog in this repo): list, skills-list, skills-new, agents-list, agents-new, fragment-new, catalog-lint, catalog-verify, review.
- Harness/agent-facing (either repo, drives the loop): worktree, verify, policy, decompose, loop, runner.
Proposal: encode the split in --help via argparse epilog sections (three grouped lists with one-liners) — no renames, nothing breaking.

UPDATE COMMAND: not needed — re-running install is the documented upgrade path (update was removed pre-release, basicly-zrj.12.1); the readme now says so explicitly.

OUTPUT STYLE, two options:
(a) stdlib-only polish: consistent status glyphs (wrote/removed/unchanged/error), aligned summary line per command (e.g. 'skills: 2 written, 50 unchanged, 1 pruned'), a single install epilogue table of what converged, dim/bold via ANSI only when stdout is a TTY. No new dependency.
(b) rich-based TUI: real tables for list/skills-list, progress spinners for install steps, colored diff-style drift reports for check/skills-check. Requires adding the 'rich' dependency — needs explicit approval per repo policy.
Recommendation: (a) first (cheap, portable, no dependency decision), (b) only if the plain version still feels flat.

Open for the user: pick (a)/(b) and approve the help-grouping approach; then this decomposes into per-command tasks.

title: DX: stop the recurring markdownlint MD060 aligned-table re-pad friction in docs
description:
Recurring DX friction (hit at least three times: v0.2.0 release tables, basicly-h0f0, and noted again in basicly-yw28 prep). markdownlint MD060 uses the "aligned" table style, so editing any cell in a wide docs table (e.g. the command table in docs/architecture.md, ~490 chars wide) requires re-padding every pipe to match the header column widths, and `markdownlint-cli2 --fix` does NOT auto-repad the aligned style. Widening the FIRST column cascades a re-pad to every row. The reliable fix each time is a hand-run Python script that reads the header pipe columns and rebuilds the edited row.

Pick a durable fix so this stops recurring. Candidate directions: (a) add a small committed `.scripts/` helper that re-pads a markdown table to MD060-aligned style (invoked manually or as a pre-commit auto-fix); (b) relax MD060 for docs (switch the config to a non-aligned table style, or disable MD060) accepting ragged pipes; (c) split the offending wide tables so cells stay short. Evaluate the trade-off (aligned tables read better in raw markdown but cost edit friction).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Editing a wide docs table no longer requires a hand-authored re-pad script: either a committed helper does it, or the lint rule no longer demands aligned pipes.
- The chosen approach is applied to docs/architecture.md's wide tables and markdownlint passes.
- Decision (helper vs relax-rule vs split-tables) recorded.

title: beads-commit-msg id detection should be prefix-anchored like beads own scanner
description:
beads-commit-msg.py validates issue-id references with a too-loose regex '\b[a-z][a-z0-9]*-[a-z0-9]+\b' that matches ANY hyphenated word, not just the configured beads prefix. The beads rust tool (br orphans) scans commits with a prefix-anchored pattern '\b(<prefix>)-[a-z0-9]+(?:\.[0-9]+)*\b', matching the id token by word boundary anywhere in the message (trailing parenthetical or footer alike). Because our hook is looser, when a commit lacks a matching known id it reports a confusing 'unknown beads issue id: fork-drove, the-loop' listing hyphenated description words as offenders, obscuring the real cause (no valid trailing id). Investigation (br 0.2.16 binary) confirms beads matches the id anywhere by prefix, so the current trailing-paren format is fine and no footer migration is needed. Fix: align our hook's id detection with beads' prefix-anchored scanner, deriving the prefix set from the known ids.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Write a commit whose description has a hyphenated multi-word phrase (e.g. fork-drove-the-loop) and omit or typo the trailing beads id
2. beads-commit-msg rejects with 'unknown beads issue id: fork-drove, the-loop', naming description words as ids
3. The real cause (no valid trailing id present) is obscured by the red-herring candidates

## Acceptance Criteria

* beads-commit-msg detects candidate ids with a prefix-anchored pattern mirroring beads' own scanner, so non-prefix hyphenated words are never treated as ids
* the prefix set is derived from the known ids with no new configuration
* a commit with a valid trailing id passes regardless of hyphenated description words; a missing id yields the no-id error; a typo'd prefix id (basicly-nope) yields an accurate unknown-id error naming only that token
* regression tests cover the valid-id-with-hyphens pass and the missing-id and typo cases

title: Guardrails: inject --deny-tool flags on the copilot runner (invocation-time deny)
description:
From basicly-u0zg.2 (closed infeasible). GitHub Copilot CLI has NO config-file deny mechanism (deny is --deny-tool CLI-flag-only, session-scoped, never persisted; permissions-config.json stores allows only; github/copilot-cli#2398 unshipped). So the baseline deny-list cannot be projected as managed catalog output for copilot. The only enforcement is invocation-time: extend the copilot runner command template to inject --deny-tool flags derived from permissions.yaml. Belongs at the runner seam (overlaps basicly-45ld). Render-testable via runner dry-run.

Verified 2026-07-18 against copilot CLI v1.0.70: --deny-tool[=tools...] takes copilot-native tool specs and matches shell commands by PREFIX, e.g. --deny-tool='shell(git push --force)' (does not block plain 'git push'); tool names like 'write'. No infix wildcards, so the fine-grained claude glob deny-list maps only PARTIALLY - a documented best-effort projection.

## Acceptance Criteria

- permissions.yaml deny rules gain an optional copilot field of copilot-native --deny-tool specs, authored only for rules copilot can meaningfully express (destructive-rm, force-push, bypass-gates, destructive-git); rules with no faithful copilot form (env-read/env-edit) are omitted with a comment.
- permissions.py exposes the flat de-duplicated copilot deny specs (mirroring claude_deny_patterns).
- The copilot runner command injects one --deny-tool=<spec> per spec, placed after the binary and before -p, populated from the repo permissions.yaml at runner-config load.
- runner dry-run --runner copilot renders the exact argv including the --deny-tool flags; a non-copilot runner is unchanged.
- The prefix-match limitation (variants/flag-reordering not all caught; env rules omitted) is documented where the mapping is authored.
- Deterministic tests cover the spec loader, format_command injection, and the dry-run surface.

title: Tool-usage counting hook projected to Claude and Copilot
description:
Deterministic, token-free usage telemetry: count which terminal tools (rg, fd, bat, jq, yq, uv, ...) the agent actually invokes, so idle skills/tools can be culled from the catalog with real data. Requested 2026-07-16; design delegated and settled below after researching both platforms.

## Design
- New catalog hook script .basicly/core/hooks/tool-usage.py (stdlib only, never blocks: every path ends exit 0). Reads the hook JSON from stdin, accepts BOTH shapes: Claude Code PostToolUse (tool_name == Bash, tool_input.command) and Copilot postToolUse (toolName/tool_name in {bash, Bash, shell}, toolArgs/tool_input command field). Splits the command string on shell operators (pipes, &&, ||, ;, newlines), takes each segment head token, skips env assignments and shell builtins (cd, echo, exit, ...), normalizes to basename.
- Persistence: .basicly/usage/tool-usage.json mapping tool -> {count, last_used date}; atomic replace (tmp + os.replace), tolerant of a missing/corrupt file (restart from empty). The hook writes .basicly/usage/.gitignore containing '*' on first run (self-ignoring dir, like .beads) so the data survives sessions but never enters git. Per-repo-folder by design (worktrees accumulate their own).
- Claude wiring: hooks.yaml entry (id tool-usage, manager claude, stage posttooluse, matcher Bash). claude_settings.py currently hardcodes PreToolUse + the Edit|Write matcher — generalize: derive the settings event from the spec stage (pretooluse -> PreToolUse, posttooluse -> PostToolUse) and add an optional matcher field to hooks.yaml (default stays the current write-tools matcher).
- Copilot wiring: hooks live in .github/hooks/*.json ({"version": 1, "hooks": {"postToolUse": [{type command, bash/powershell/command keys, optional matcher}]}}). Add manager 'copilot' to HOOK_MANAGERS with a projection to .github/hooks/basicly-tool-usage.json (bash + powershell keys both run the same python script via uv), wired into hooks-build/hooks-check/uninstall like the claude manager.
- Dogfood: the hook ships in this repo's own projections (hooks-build here).

## Non-functional
- Zero tokens: pure python, no model calls. Resilient: never fails the tool call (async-safe, exit 0 always), atomic writes, corrupt-file recovery. Survives sessions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Piping a recorded Claude PostToolUse Bash JSON into the script increments counts for each pipeline segment head (e.g. 'rg foo | jq .x' -> rg+1, jq+1) and writes the self-ignoring .gitignore
- The Copilot stdin shape is parsed by the same script (unit test with both payload shapes)
- A corrupt or missing usage file never fails the hook (exit 0) and the next write starts clean
- hooks-build projects the Claude PostToolUse group into .claude/settings.json and the Copilot hook file into .github/hooks/, both idempotent; hooks-check flags drift; uninstall removes both
- Existing pretooluse protect-generated behavior is unchanged (matcher default preserved)
- Dogfooded: this repo's projections carry the hook after hooks-build; git status stays clean after a counted command runs
- Regression tests cover parsing, counting, atomicity fallback, both stdin shapes, and both projections

title: Probe br redirect capability at worktree provisioning
description:
Team-rollout hardening for basicly-c9e5: the harness loop now depends on br honoring .beads/redirect - the first br CAPABILITY dependency (not just presence). An older br silently ignores the redirect, auto-imports the worktree's checked-out issues.jsonl into a fresh local DB, and runs a divergent tracker (lost gates/claims) with no error. Add a behavior probe to worktree provisioning: after writing the redirect (moved early, before the slow dep install), run br where --json from the worktree; if br runs successfully but resolves a path other than the base .beads, abort provisioning with guidance (br upgrade, floor br 0.2.16 known good, cleanup command). A missing br or a non-workspace .beads skips the probe - br-less repos remain a supported state. Document the br floor in architecture 12.6.1.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Provisioning fails with a clear upgrade message when br resolves the worktree .beads instead of following the redirect (test), skips the probe when br is absent or errors (test), and passes silently on a redirect-capable br (existing tests). The redirect+probe step runs before dependency provisioning so the failure is fast. architecture.md names the br redirect capability as a loop requirement with 0.2.16 as the known-good floor. Full gate suite passes.

title: Harden loop ship against an unmerged worktree and document the hand-recorded gate trap
description:
Retro from basicly-45ld: recording the verify gate out-of-band (basicly verify --issue / br gate report) makes derive_phase jump past the build->verify landing (loop._on_build -> _verify_and_land), which is the only step that merges the worktree. The loop then shipped+closed the bead with the feature commit stranded on the harness branch, unmerged. Two complementary fixes.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Engine guard: loop._on_ship refuses to close/teardown a leaf whose bound worktree branch is not merged into its base (branch tip not an ancestor of base HEAD); it blocks with a clear reason naming the branch and base instead of closing. A branch that no longer exists (already merged + cleaned) is treated as merged. The guard runs before worktree.cleanup and before br close.
- Deterministic and side-effect-free on the block path: no close, no teardown, no tracker commit when the guard trips.
- Regression tests: a bound-but-unmerged worktree blocks at ship (issue stays open, not closed); a merged worktree ships normally; a missing branch ships normally.
- Skill doc: the harness-loop skill warns never to record the verify gate by hand during an active leaf build because the build->verify advance is the only step that merges the worktree; let loop advance record it. Projected SKILL.md regenerated.
- pytest, ruff, and the projection checks (skills-check) stay green.

## Scope

loop._on_ship guard + a small reachability helper + tests; harness-loop skill.yaml note + skills-build. No change to merge.py, no new gate mechanism.

title: Retrieval: structured I-dont-know / needs-input as a first-class loop outcome
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 6 (D5/D6 convergence). basicly's context contribution is static projection; the "I don't know" contract is soft prose in the knowledge-priming/decision-protocol fragments the model may ignore. The loop owns a hard block-and-resume outcome (`_blocked` in loop.py) but it fires only on runner failure/checkpoints/gates, never on the agent itself signalling "iteration budget spent, fact not found".

Make a structured needs-input / I-don't-know a first-class loop outcome the HEADLESS runner can signal, mapping to the loop's existing block-and-resume contract (`_blocked(..., needs_input=<fact>)`) so the loop blocks and surfaces the missing fact instead of landing a confident wrong answer.

Signal carrier (decided): a self-ignored sentinel file the agent writes into its worktree, `.basicly/usage/needs-input.json` = {"fact": ..., "detail": ...}. The loop reads it after a clean (exit 0) dispatch, blocks with needs_input, and consumes it. Robust against output redaction/truncation and agent-neutral. The manual/handoff runner is unchanged (the driver already surfaces missing facts); a manual-driver CLI is explicitly out of scope.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A headless dispatch that finishes exit 0 with a valid `.basicly/usage/needs-input.json` in the worktree blocks the loop with a needs-input result (populated `needs_input`), does NOT land the worktree, and consumes (removes) the sentinel so a re-dispatch starts clean.
- A malformed or empty sentinel is tolerated (no crash) and falls through to the normal "finished; advance again to land it" block; a missing sentinel keeps today's behaviour exactly.
- The dispatch prompt and a projected catalog fragment document the protocol (when to write the sentinel and its shape), turning the prose escalation policy into an enforced seam.
- The needs-input reason/fact is surfaced by `loop status` / advance output the same way other blocks are.
- Full suite, ruff, ruff format, pyright, bandit, and projection gates (basicly check / skills-check / agents-check / hooks-check) pass; verified by exercising a real dispatch (not just unit tests).

## Notes

- Scope is the headless dispatch path in `loop._dispatch_runner`; the sentinel lives in the already self-ignored `.basicly/usage/` dir (same convention as run-records, tool-usage, checkpoint-confirms).

title: Fix env deny-list to Edit-only form Claude Code accepts

title: Claude target install: set worktree bg-isolation to none for harness worktrees

title: drive the checkpoint-gated loop state machine
description:
## Acceptance Criteria

- advances intake to classify to decompose to build to merge to verify to ship to teardown
- enforces the three human checkpoints and the bounded rework loop via the policy engine
- composes the decomposer, worktree lifecycle, verify runner, and serial merge queue

- `src/basicly/loop.py`
- `tests/test_loop.py`
close_reason: Implemented the checkpoint-gated loop state machine (src/basicly/loop.py): a resumable single-step advance() that reads the current phase from br (loop_state, onb.6.1) and dispatches per phase, composing classify (onb.6.2), the decomposer (onb.4), worktree lifecycle (onb.1), the verify runner (onb.2), and the serial merge queue (onb.5), enforcing the three human checkpoints and the bounded rework loop via the policy engine (onb.3). Key correctness property: because the phase is derived from br, every step either blocks or produces a new br signal that moves the derived phase forward, so handlers and derive_phase never disagree and run_until_blocked cannot spin. Leaf types build in their own worktree; features fan out child worktrees and land them via the merge queue. AdvanceResult reports from/to/action/blocked/needs_input; run_until_blocked driver over advance(). 208 tests pass (18 new); dogfooded on a real throwaway feature (intake records type, blocks on classify checkpoint; fresh advance after approval reconstructs classify phase purely from br and blocks needing children) then hard-deleted. 3rd of onb.6's four children.
