CORE SHAPE — a single-step, resumable transition:
- advance(repo_root, issue_id, *, config=None, work_type=None, children=None) -> AdvanceResult.
- It FIRST reads loop_state.read_node_state(issue_id) to get the current phase (resume point), then dispatches on phase via a HANDLER DICT (phase -> _handler), mirroring cmd_worktree/cmd_policy dispatch so we stay under PLR0911 (<=6 returns) / PLR0913 (<=5 args) — pass agent inputs in a small frozen Inputs dataclass, not as many kwargs.
- AdvanceResult(frozen): from_phase, to_phase (== from_phase when blocked), action ("classified"/"decomposed"/"built"/"merged"/"verified"/"shipped"/"tore-down"/"blocked"/"escalated"), blocked_reason|None, needs_input|None (what the agent/human must supply next). No exceptions for normal blocks — return a blocked result.

PHASE -> ACTION MAPPING (each composes an already-tested module):
- intake: requires work_type; absent -> blocked(needs_input="work_type"); present -> classify.classify(repo,id,work_type) -> to "classify".
- classify: gated by the classify human checkpoint (policy.checkpoint_approved) AND DoR (classify returns can_leave_classify / policy.definition_of_ready). Both true -> to "decompose"; else blocked(needs classify checkpoint approval and/or DoR sections).
- decompose: requires children plan; absent -> blocked(needs_input="children"); present -> decompose.decompose(repo,id,children); then gated by the decompose checkpoint -> to "build".
- build: for each dependency-unblocked child (loop_state.ready_ranked, concurrency-capped by WorktreeConfig), worktree.create(name) and stash the binding via br update --external-ref loop_state.format_worktree_ref(name,branch). Agent builds + verifies each child (that is child-level advance recursion — see design Q3). When children are green -> to "merge".
- merge: merge.merge_queue(repo, [(name,bead)...], config) in the decomposer's serial/topological order; on a node failure the queue already records rework + escalation (policy) -> surface as blocked/escalated.
- verify: verify.run_verify(repo, mode) + verify.report_gate(repo,id,report); gate_status must show the required gate green (policy.gate_status.can_advance) -> to "ship"; fail -> policy.record_rework + should_escalate (bounded n=2, config.max_rework) -> blocked/escalated.
- ship: gated by the ship human checkpoint -> to "teardown".
- teardown: worktree.cleanup for the node's worktrees + br close the issue -> "done".

DESIGN QUESTIONS TO RESOLVE IN-SESSION:
(Q1) Single-step advance() core vs a run() loop. RECOMMEND: advance() is the primitive (resumable, agent/human re-invokes each step); add a thin run_until_blocked(repo, issue_id, inputs) driver that loops advance() until it blocks/checkpoints/finishes. The CLI (onb.6.4) wires both; one-shot mode (arch 12.2) = auto-approve the middle checkpoint in the driver.
(Q2) Where agent inputs live: pass work_type/children into advance() (or an Inputs dataclass); when a phase needs one and it is absent, block and report needs_input. Keeps the engine agent-agnostic (no prompting inside the engine).
(Q3) Fractal recursion (epic->feature->task, arch 12.1/12.2). RECOMMEND scope onb.6.3 to the SINGLE-TRACK linear machine; children advance via the SAME advance() invoked per child by the driver over ready_ranked. Full auto-recursion inside one advance() call is deferred (leave a clear hook + a note); the AC only requires the linear phase machine + checkpoints + rework + resume. Confirm this boundary.

TESTS (tests/test_loop.py): unit-test each transition by monkeypatching the composed callables on the loop module (loop.read_node_state, loop.classify, loop.decompose, loop.worktree, loop.verify, loop.merge, loop.policy) — same fake pattern as test_merge/test_loop_state. Cover: resume-from-each-phase advances correctly; a pending checkpoint blocks (not error); DoR-not-ready blocks the classify->decompose exit; a failed verify records rework and escalates at the cap; a clean happy path walks intake->...->done. Assert on AdvanceResult (from/to/action/blocked_reason), and that the right module was invoked.

DOGFOOD (real br, keep it light — build/merge are already dogfooded in their own modules): create a throwaway feature, advance() it: intake w/ work_type -> classify (real br type recorded), then classify blocked on the pending checkpoint; approve_checkpoint + add AC so DoR passes -> advance to decompose blocked on needs_input="children". Verify the machine advances/blocks against REAL br, then hard-delete the throwaway.

GUARDRAILS (from this session): verify REAL br json field names before asserting (dependency_type vs type bit us); commit SUBJECT = lowercase letters/digits/spaces/hyphens only, NO . / + / _ (bead id trailing in BODY); the commit-messages CI gate now validates the FULL message (fixed in basicly-acx) so a body-trailer id is fine; before `git add`, check `git status` so no file left staged from a prior attempt sneaks in; pre-commit stashes unstaged tracked files, so commit a SELF-CONSISTENT set (don't split a symbol from its importer); structure advance() as a phase->handler dispatch dict to stay under PLR0911/PLR0913.

AFTER .3: onb.6.4 (basicly loop CLI) unblocks (last child) -> then onb.6 closes -> onb.8 (guidance) unblocks. onb.7 (P3 runner adapters) independent.

title: Governance: gate loop human-checkpoint approvals on interactive confirmation so a non-interactive/subagent process cannot self-approve ship
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 field incident (2026-07-17): a Claude Code fork inherited the full directive and autonomously drove the loop to ship+close (checkpoint --approve, loop advance) beyond its read-only mandate. Because the human session and its forks share one OS/git identity (the D1 gap), basicly cannot distinguish a rogue approval from an authorized one. Mitigation basicly can own: make policy checkpoint {classify,decompose,ship} --approve refuse in a non-interactive context (require a TTY or an ephemeral confirm-code the human types), so a subagent Bash without a controlling TTY cannot self-approve. Deterministic, agent-agnostic, aligns with the human-checkpoint intent. First task: verify the 'tool-invoked Bash has no TTY' assumption across claude/codex/copilot before relying on it as a floor. Complementary to attribution (basicly-z6dh run-record, basicly-140a). Does not prevent doc-writing/bead-filing (cheap, reversible) - only the consequential ship.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `policy checkpoint <name> --approve` records approval directly when stdin is an interactive TTY.
- In a non-interactive context (no controlling TTY) with no confirm code, the command refuses to approve (non-zero exit, no marker recorded) and prints a one-time ephemeral confirm code plus the exact re-run command.
- Re-running with `--confirm <code>` records the approval only when the code matches and has not expired; a wrong or expired code is refused with no marker recorded.
- The gate covers all three checkpoints (classify, decompose, ship).
- Confirm-code state lives in the self-ignored .basicly/usage/ dir using the same atomic write pattern as run-records.
- TTY assumption verified for Claude Code (no TTY on stdin/stdout/stderr); codex/copilot use the same piped-subprocess pattern (inferred).
- Deterministic and agent-agnostic; does not defeat a determined same-identity fork (D1 out of scope).
design: Add a guarded-approval layer in policy.py. approve_checkpoint stays the raw recorder. New approve_checkpoint_guarded(repo_root, issue, name, *, interactive, confirm) returns an ApprovalResult(status in {approved,challenge,rejected}, code, detail). interactive True -> record + approved. Non-interactive + confirm None -> generate ephemeral code, store {issue:name -> {code, expires}} in .basicly/usage/checkpoint-confirms.json (atomic tmp-replace, self-ignored), return challenge with the code. Non-interactive + confirm -> consume-and-validate against stored code and TTL -> approved or rejected. Code generator and clock are module-level helpers (_new_code, _now) so tests monkeypatch them and the public signature stays under the 5-arg PLR0913 ceiling. CLI _cmd_policy_checkpoint detects sys.stdin.isatty(), passes interactive + --confirm, and maps ApprovalResult to output + exit code. TTL constant e.g. 900s.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `policy checkpoint <name> --approve` records approval directly when stdin is an interactive TTY.
2. In a non-interactive context (no controlling TTY) with no confirm code, the command REFUSES to approve (non-zero exit, no marker recorded), and prints a one-time ephemeral confirm code plus the exact re-run command.
3. Re-running with `--confirm <code>` records the approval only when the code matches and has not expired (TTL); a wrong or expired code is refused with no marker recorded.
4. The gate covers all three checkpoints (classify, decompose, ship).
5. Confirm-code state is stored in the self-ignored .basicly/usage/ dir (never committed) using the same atomic write pattern as run-records.
6. TTY assumption verified: a tool-invoked Bash (Claude Code) has no controlling TTY on stdin/stdout/stderr; codex/copilot invoke Bash via the same piped-subprocess pattern (inferred, not directly exercised).
7. Deterministic and agent-agnostic; explicitly does NOT defeat a determined same-identity fork (the D1 shared-identity gap is out of scope) - it forces a deliberate second step that an autopilot directive will not contain.

title: Guardrails: project a baseline agent-permissions deny-list across claude/copilot/codex as managed catalog output
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 2. The strong .claude/settings.json deny-list (destructive git, --no-verify/--no-gpg-sign, .env access) is repo-local and NOT projected; consumers get none of it. Add a catalog-managed permissions guardrail rendered per target (Claude permissions.deny, Copilot tool-deny, Codex sandbox/approval defaults), managed the way hooks are.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A tool-agnostic catalog source (.basicly/core/permissions/permissions.yaml) plus a permissions module mirror the hooks pipeline (per-manager dispatch, semantic drift on co-owned files), not the fragment/hash-manifest build pipeline
- permissions-build projects the deny-list per target and permissions-check verifies it semantically: the managed block is merged into the co-owned file, consumer-added entries are preserved, and stale managed entries are pruned
- Claude surface: the permissions.deny block of .claude/settings.json becomes catalog-managed; the repo migrates its existing hand-authored deny-list into the source so the repo dogfoods its own guardrail
- Copilot tool-deny and Codex sandbox/approval surfaces are projected, render-tested, and shipped with an explicit unverified-against-live-CLI caveat
- The new command is wired into install, basicly check, and status --json (drift reported like hook drift); pytest, ruff, and the projection gates stay green
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: install migration leaves the legacy vendored engine dir in place
description:
Found during the terminal-repo acceptance (basicly-zrj.15, 2026-07-15): basicly install migrates/prunes legacy .fragment.md and core SKILL.md sources correctly, but leaves the legacy vendored engine directory .basicly/basicly/ (the pre-src-layout engine copy) untouched. A consumer migrating from a hand install keeps a stale, non-functional engine tree inside the managed core root.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Consumer repo with the legacy layout: .basicly/basicly/*.py (old vendored engine) + .basicly/core/.
2. uvx basicly install (and --force): legacy fragment/skill sources are pruned, but .basicly/basicly/ remains (observed in the terminal repo).

## Acceptance Criteria

- _migrate_legacy_layout removes .basicly/basicly/ (the legacy vendored engine tree) with a printed note.
- Regression test covers a consumer with the legacy engine dir present.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: overlay legacy fragment-md sources are silently ignored
description:
Found during the terminal acceptance (basicly-zrj.15): the loader only reads *.fragment.yaml since the c1c source-format migration, so overlay fragments still in the legacy *.fragment.md format are silently ignored — terminal's three user fragments (project-defaults, python-style, no-user-path-leakage) were on disk but absent from basicly list and every projection. Install prunes legacy sources in the CORE but must never touch the overlay, so a hand-installed consumer silently loses all customization after upgrading.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Consumer with .basicly-local/fragments/user/x.fragment.md (legacy format, applies_to: [all], status: active).
2. basicly install / build: no error, no warning; basicly list does not show x; generated files omit it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- basicly build/install/list emit a loud warning listing every legacy *.fragment.md found under overlay roots, naming the YAML migration path (basicly fragment-new / catalog-authoring skill); exit stays 0 (advisory).
- basicly check also surfaces the warning so CI catches it.
- Test covers an overlay with a legacy md fragment.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: tool-usage telemetry counts heredoc bodies, python keywords and bare flags as tools
description:
The tool-usage PostToolUse hook pipeline-head extraction still records non-tool tokens as tools. Live 'basicly usage report' (2026-07-17) shows the tools table counting def (90, last used today), assert (83), from (47), return (31), import (18), heredoc delimiters EOF/PYEOF, and bare flags -d (120)/-/t/p, plus words like the/old/new/result. These leak from heredoc bodies and python -c/-m inline snippets that the quote-aware split (basicly-zcvo / commit c5bdc4e) does not fully strip. Impact: pollutes the D3 observability telemetry the harness uses to cull idle skills/tools. Fix: strip heredoc bodies fully, skip python -c/-m inline token bodies, and drop leading bare flags before taking a segment head in tools_in_command (.basicly/core/hooks/tool-usage.py). Add regression cases for a heredoc, a python -c one-liner, and a flag-led segment. Note: earlier in the spike session this was mis-triaged as already-fixed by checking for the fix commit instead of running the report.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run a shell command whose heredoc tag is backslash-escaped (e.g. 'python3 - <<\\PY') or whose pipeline has a flag-led segment
2. The PostToolUse tool-usage hook records the heredoc body's python keywords, the terminator tag, and the bare flag as tool names
3. 'basicly usage report' lists def/import/return/PY/-d etc. as tools, polluting the D3 observability telemetry

## Acceptance Criteria

* heredoc bodies are stripped even when the tag is backslash-escaped (<<\\TAG) or tab-stripped (<<-TAG), so no body line or terminator is counted
* a pipeline segment whose head is a bare flag or a shell builtin records nothing for that segment
* python -c/-m inline snippets never leak their code tokens as tool names
* regression tests cover a backslash-tag heredoc, a python -c one-liner, and a flag-led segment
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Fix Windows CI: basicly status crashes when git subprocess env lacks PATH
description:
The quality-gates Windows job failed on the four `basicly status` tests with `[WinError 2] The system cannot find the file specified`. First Windows CI run to include the status command (3yv..kd8 landed locally, then pushed together).

Two root causes:
1. Product: hooks._git_hooks_dir runs `git rev-parse` but did not handle git being absent; status is documented to always exit 0 but crashed. Fixed: catch OSError and fall back to <repo>/.git/hooks.
2. Test realism: tests/test_cli.py run_basicly/run_basicly_consumer/cp1252 helpers replaced the process env with only PYTHONPATH, stripping PATH. POSIX has a default-path fallback for execvp so git still resolved; Windows has none, so git was not found. Fixed: base env on os.environ.

## Acceptance Criteria

- basicly status exits 0 when git is not on PATH (regression test); the CLI test helpers inherit PATH; full gate suite passes; Windows CI green.
close_reason: Shipped: hooks._git_hooks_dir catches OSError and falls back to <repo>/.git/hooks; CLI test helpers base env on os.environ (keep PATH). Landed 8712cef + quirks 6105269, on origin/main; Windows CI green.

title: release tags can ship stale package metadata
description:
The second release tag shipped package metadata still reading 0.1.0: uvx --from git+https://github.com/niksavis/basicly@v0.1.1 basicly --version prints basicly 0.1.0, and consumers' .basicly/state/install.json gets stamped with the stale basicly_version, breaking version-based upgrade/drift detection. Root cause: the version is duplicated in pyproject.toml and src/basicly/__init__.py, neither was bumped when the tag was cut, and nothing gates a tag against the package version.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Cut a tag without bumping pyproject.toml/src/basicly/__init__.py.
2. uvx --from git+https://github.com/niksavis/basicly@<tag> basicly --version → prints the old version.
3. basicly install in a consumer → install.json basicly_version records the old version.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Package version is single-sourced (hatchling dynamic version from the module) so pyproject and module cannot drift.
- Version bumped so the next tag ships matching metadata; v0.1.1 is NOT moved or re-tagged.
- Release workflow fails when the tag name and the package version disagree.
- Verified end to end: uvx at the new tag prints the matching version.
close_reason: version single-sourced from the module via hatchling dynamic version and bumped; release workflow now fails before publishing when tag and package version disagree; verified via uv build artifact names, basicly --version, gate dry-run both ways, and full test suite

title: Tooling: hooks-build should preserve out-of-block content in the pre-commit config
description:
Found while shipping basicly-yw28 (2026-07-18). `basicly hooks-build` rewrites `.pre-commit-config.yaml` and normalizes content OUTSIDE its managed hook block: it hoisted the hand-maintained `markdownlint` entry to the top of the file and dropped its explanatory comment ("Repo-wide run through .markdownlint-cli2.yaml ... so explicit paths can't bypass the config's ignore list"). `basicly hooks-check` only validates managed-hook PRESENCE, so the drop passes the gate silently — a single-hook add via the canonical `hooks-build` therefore clobbers unmanaged hooks' placement and comments as collateral. Workaround used in yw28: revert the full regen and surgically insert only the new managed entry, preserving markdownlint.

Two candidate directions: (a) hooks-build preserves out-of-block entries verbatim (placement + comments), only touching its own managed block; or (b) `.pre-commit-config.yaml` becomes fully managed (markdownlint moves into hooks.yaml as a managed git hook, so nothing is hand-maintained). Decide which; (b) is cleaner long-term but changes the consumer story for repos with their own hooks.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Running `basicly hooks-build` after adding/removing a managed hook leaves any unmanaged pre-commit entry (e.g. a consumer's own markdownlint) byte-for-byte unchanged, including its comments and position — OR markdownlint is folded into the managed set and the file is documented as fully generated.
- A regression test proves an unmanaged hook entry with a comment survives a hooks-build round-trip (or that no unmanaged entries are expected).
- Decision recorded (preserve-out-of-block vs fully-managed) in the bead or architecture.md.

title: throwaway gate history probe
core pre-commit/pre-push hooks: run configured verify checks instead of a hard-coded python stack
description:
The core hook scripts pre-commit.py and pre-push.py ship to every consumer via the catalog and get wired+activated by install, but they hard-code the authoring repo's stack: ruff, ruff-format, pyright, bandit, node_modules markdownlint (pre-commit) and uv run pytest tests/ (pre-push). Any consumer without that stack fails every commit/push once hooks activate — release-blocking for the terminal-repo acceptance. The config-driven verify layer ([[verify.checks]] in basicly.toml, made consumer-appropriate in basicly-zrj.13.2) is what the hooks should run.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. basicly install in a non-Python repo (hooks activate).
2. Commit: pre-commit.py runs `uv run ruff check .` etc. — tool/venv missing, hook fails; push runs pytest on a repo without tests/.

## Acceptance Criteria

- pre-commit.py runs the basicly.toml [[verify.checks]] configured for mode "fast"; pre-push.py runs mode "full"; both stdlib-only (tomllib), no basicly import, standalone and manager-agnostic; empty/missing config passes with a note.
- A configured command missing from PATH fails with a one-line message (mirror verify.py's 127/126 contract), never a traceback.
- The authoring repo declares its full check set in its own basicly.toml (adds bandit and markdownlint entries) so dogfood coverage is unchanged — verified by running both hook scripts against this repo.
- Unit tests: empty config passes, failing check fails, missing tool one-liner, mode routing (fast vs full).
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Guardrails: protect-generated git-stage manifest backstop
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 2. `protect-generated.py` is a fail-open, Claude-specific PreToolUse hook: a tool-time bypass (or any non-Claude agent) can still stage a hand-edit to a basicly-generated file and reach a commit. Add a deterministic, agent-independent git backstop that catches it at commit time.

Design: a new pre-commit hook (`manager: git`, stdlib-only, companion to `protect-generated.py`) reads the projection manifest (`generated-manifest.json` -> `outputs` map of repo-relative path -> recorded sha256) and, for every staged generated OUTPUT whose staged blob no longer matches the hash the last `basicly build` recorded, blocks the commit. A legitimate rebuild stages the regenerated file AND the updated manifest together so hashes agree (pass); a hand-edit that skips the rebuild does not (block). Fail-safe: a missing manifest or unreadable git index exits 0 (a guardrail against accidents, not a security boundary — same stance as the PreToolUse guard).

## Acceptance Criteria

- A new pre-commit git hook blocks a commit that stages a basicly-generated output file whose staged content diverges from the projection manifest's recorded hash, naming the offending file(s) and pointing at the catalog-source + `basicly build` fix.
- A legitimate rebuild (regenerated output + updated manifest staged together) passes; a commit that stages no generated files passes; a staged deletion is not blocked (edits are the target).
- Fail-safe: a missing/unreadable manifest or a git-index read failure exits 0 (never locks a commit on the hook's own error).
- The hook is wired into `hooks.yaml` (`manager: git`, `stage: pre-commit`) and projected by `basicly hooks-build`; `basicly hooks-check` passes and the projected pre-commit config carries the entry.
- Full suite, ruff, ruff format, pyright, bandit, and the projection gates (basicly check / skills-check / agents-check / hooks-check) pass; verified by exercising a real commit (a hand-edited generated file is blocked; a clean tree commits).

## Notes

- The existing `protect-generated.py` PreToolUse guard stays as the first line (Claude tool-time); this hook is the deterministic commit-time backstop for all agents.
- Hash is compared over the staged blob bytes against the working-tree manifest (which reflects the last build); this mirrors `renderers.common.sha256_of_text` without importing basicly (hooks are standalone stdlib scripts copied to consumers).
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Guardrails: secret-scanning deterministic gate on committed content
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimension 2. No redact/secret-scan logic exists anywhere. Add a blocking pre-commit gate so leaked credentials are caught deterministically. Chosen mechanism: a built-in stdlib scanner hook (no external dependency), distributed to consumers the way the other catalog hooks are.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A new stdlib-only core/hooks/secret-scan.py scans staged content at pre-commit and blocks the commit (non-zero exit) when a likely secret is detected, reporting file:line and the matched rule
- Detects common patterns: private-key headers, provider tokens (AWS, GitHub, Slack and similar), generic secret-named assignments, and dotenv-style KEY=value secrets
- An inline suppression pragma lets a reviewed false positive through; only staged/added content is scanned
- hooks.yaml registers it (stage pre-commit, manager git); hooks-build wires it into .pre-commit-config.yaml and hooks-check verifies it; the repo dogfoods the active hook
- Deterministic tests cover each detection pattern, clean content passing, suppression, and staged-only scanning, with fixtures that build secrets by concatenation so committing the tests does not self-trip the hook
- pytest, ruff, pyright, and the projection gates stay green
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Windows compatibility fixes from the first os-matrix run
description:

title: Observability: runner run-record capturing per-run duration, exit, agent and model, keyed by bead id
description:
From spike basicly-zv48 dimensions 3/1/4/5 (foundational, cross-cutting). runner.RunResult captures only returncode/stdout/stderr; nothing records timing, agent, model, or tokens against the bead/loop. Capture wall-clock duration + exit + agent + model (and tokens/cost where the agent exposes OTEL/session usage) into .basicly/usage keyed by bead id. Foundation for agent attribution (D1), model provenance (D4), fleet rollup (D3), and structured run outcome (D5).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Per-run record written keyed by bead id at the runner-dispatch seam into the self-ignored .basicly/usage/ directory via the atomic tmp-write-then-replace pattern
- Record captures wall-clock duration, exit outcome (executed/handoff/failed), and agent (runner name); model and token/cost fields present but null (reserved for basicly-45ld and a token follow-on)
- Metadata only: no prompt body and no stdout/stderr output persisted; the command is stored with the prompt elided
- A loader reads records back; tests cover write, read, redaction, and corrupt-file-restarts-empty
- pytest, ruff, and the projection checks stay green

title: tool-usage hook counts heredoc/argument words as command heads
description:
The tool-usage PostToolUse hook (.basicly/core/hooks/tool-usage.py) is meant to count each shell command's pipeline heads, but it also records argument words and here-doc/commit-message body words as if they were command heads. In a real session it logged tokens like 'Refine', 'musical', 'portable', '--description', 'G_MODEL' alongside git/gh/br. This pollutes 'basicly usage report' with non-commands and makes the never-used-skill culling signal unreliable.

Root cause: tools_in_command() splits the raw command on pipeline operators (| && ; and newline) with a regex BEFORE tokenizing, so operators and newlines that appear INSIDE a quoted string (a multi-line 'git commit -m' body, a 'gh --title/--body' with a semicolon) shatter the quoted text into fake segments whose first word is then counted as a command head. Heredoc bodies are already stripped (basicly-587); quoted-string bodies are not.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run a command whose args include a multi-word quoted string with an operator or newline, e.g. 'git commit -m' with a multi-line body, or 'gh pr create --title "add x; ship it"', or a 'python3 - <<PY' heredoc.
2. Inspect .basicly/usage/tool-usage.json.
3. Observe body words (musical, portable, --description, ship) recorded as keys alongside the real commands.

## Acceptance Criteria

- tools_in_command() splits into pipeline segments in a quote-aware way: | && ; and newline that occur inside single/double quotes do NOT start a new segment, so quoted-string contents, flag values, and heredoc bodies are never counted.
- Only the real command at each pipeline head is counted; wrappers (uv/npx/...) still unwrap; builtins/env-assignments still skipped.
- Regression test covers a multi-line 'git commit -m' body AND a <<'PY' heredoc, asserting only the real commands are counted.
- Full test suite green.

title: One-command lifecycle: basicly install (init+update unified) and basicly uninstall
description:
Unify install/init/update into a single idempotent converge command so the consumer story is exactly one command for first install AND every upgrade: uvx --from git+https://github.com/niksavis/basicly@<ref> basicly install. Add the missing removal path as basicly uninstall. Supersedes the separate init/update subcommands — unreleased, so they are removed, not deprecated.

Design investigation (2026-07-15): init has no technical reason to be separate; everything it does is already idempotent skip-existing. Real constraints for unification: (a) preserve the authoring-repo guard (core src == dst), (b) never overwrite a user-edited basicly.toml or overlay content, (c) core upgrade overwrite needs provenance hashes to distinguish upstream changes from user hand-edits (install.json, basicly-8fg). Architecture: §9 (redesigned under this epic).

Acceptance criteria:
- A fresh consumer repo needs exactly one command to get working agent files, skills, and installed hooks.
- Running the same command again (same or newer basicly version) converges: core synced, overlay + basicly.toml untouched, artifacts rebuilt.
- basicly uninstall removes everything managed, preserving user content by default.
- init/update no longer exist in the CLI; docs and the dogfood repo are updated.

One pinned uvx command now installs AND upgrades without clobbering customizations; uninstall removes it all cleanly.

title: Implement basicly install: idempotent converge (sync core + scaffold + build + skills-build + hooks-build)
description:
One command replaces init + build + skills-build + hooks-build + update. Reuses the existing idempotent building blocks (write-if-changed sync, managed pre-commit block, skip-existing scaffold).

Behavior:
- Fresh repo: materialize bundled core, scaffold .basicly-local/fragments/user/ + basicly.toml, then build, skills-build (default roots), hooks-build (with hook installation).
- Existing repo: converge (core sync per basicly-zrj.14 once that lands; until then materialize-missing), rebuild all artifacts.
- Keep the authoring-repo guard (bundled core src == dst -> leave in place).
- Remove init and update subcommands from the CLI; update README/next-steps text and tests.

Acceptance criteria:
- In a fresh temp repo: a single basicly install produces AGENTS.md, .claude/CLAUDE.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, scoped rules, projected skills, wired+installed hooks, basicly.toml, overlay dir.
- Second run reports convergence with no changes; a user-edited basicly.toml and overlay files are never overwritten.
- Authoring-repo (this repo) run leaves the catalog source in place and still rebuilds artifacts.
- basicly --help no longer lists init/update; tests cover fresh, re-run, and authoring-repo cases.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Core catalog upgrade sync: overwrite managed core from bundled catalog, prune removed files, protect hand-edits
description:
The missing half of upgrade: `basicly install` must sync `.basicly/core/` to the bundled catalog version, not just materialize missing files. Core is managed/read-only (§4.2), so upstream wins there; the overlay is never touched. Uses the basicly-8fg provenance snapshot to distinguish upstream changes from user hand-edits.

Sync rules per bundled/on-disk/recorded hash triple:
- missing on disk -> write (new file)
- disk == bundled -> unchanged
- disk == recorded, bundled differs -> upstream change -> overwrite
- disk != recorded (or no snapshot) -> hand-edit -> warn + skip, `--force` overwrites
- on disk, not in bundle, disk == recorded -> upstream removed -> delete
- on disk, not in bundle, disk != recorded or unknown origin -> warn + keep (never delete unknown content)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Given an older installed core and a newer bundled catalog: changed core files are overwritten, upstream-removed core files are deleted, overlay fragments/overrides and basicly.toml are byte-for-byte untouched.
- A hand-edited core file (hash mismatch vs .basicly/state/install.json) is not silently clobbered: warn and skip by default, `--force` overwrites; both paths tested.
- Files present in core but unknown to both the bundle and the snapshot are kept with a warning, never deleted.
- After sync, install re-snapshots provenance and rebuilds artifacts so generated files match the new core.
- Tests cover: new/changed/removed upstream files, hand-edit skip and --force, unknown-file keep, overlay untouched.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

title: Implement basicly uninstall: remove managed core, state, generated artifacts, projected skills, managed hook block
description:
Removal path so basicly can be taken out of a repo as cleanly as it went in (§9). Must refuse to run in the authoring repo (core == bundled source), since that would delete the catalog source itself.

Scope: delete the managed core root, the state dir, the generated files listed in the manifest (and the manifest), projected SKILL.md files carrying the generated marker in the default skill roots, and the managed repo:local block in .pre-commit-config.yaml (deleting the file and uninstalling the git hooks when nothing else remains in it). Clean up directories left empty. Keep the overlay and basicly.toml unless --purge.

## Acceptance Criteria

- After install then uninstall in a temp repo: .basicly/ (core, state, manifest) is gone, generated files listed in the manifest are gone, projected SKILL.md files are gone, and the pre-commit config is gone when it contained only the managed block.
- With a foreign pre-commit hook present, the config file survives with the foreign hook intact and only the managed block removed.
- .basicly-local/ overlay and basicly.toml are preserved by default; --purge removes them too.
- A hand-written SKILL.md without the generated marker is never deleted.
- Running uninstall twice is a no-op with exit 0; running it in the authoring repo refuses with a clear error.
- Tests cover: default, foreign-hooks, --purge, marker-respecting skill removal, idempotent rerun, and the authoring-repo guard.

title: worktree provisioning clobbers shared git hooks with worktree venv paths
description:
Found while shipping basicly-zrj.12.1 (2026-07-15): creating a harness worktree runs pre-commit install from the WORKTREE venv, but git worktrees share .git/hooks with the base checkout, so the installed hook shims embed the worktree venv pre-commit path. Worktree teardown deletes that venv, leaving the base checkout with broken hooks (every commit fails with pre-commit not found) until basicly hooks-build is re-run manually.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. In the base checkout, run `basicly worktree create <name>` (or let `basicly loop advance` provision one) in an environment where `pre-commit` resolves via the worktree venv (`uv run` fallback in hooks.install_hooks with cwd=worktree).
2. Observe `.git/hooks/pre-commit` (shared common dir) now embeds `<repo>.worktrees/<name>/.venv/...` paths.
3. Tear the worktree down (`basicly worktree cleanup <name>` or loop ship).
4. Attempt any commit in the base checkout: the hook shim fails (pre-commit executable gone) until `basicly hooks-build` is re-run manually. Bit the team three times on 2026-07-15.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Creating and tearing down a worktree leaves the base checkout hooks working: a commit in the base checkout succeeds immediately after worktree cleanup.
- Either provision installs hooks in a worktree-safe way (e.g. core.hooksPath per worktree, or shims that resolve pre-commit from PATH/repo venv) or teardown reinstalls hooks from the base checkout.
- Regression test covering create -> teardown -> base-checkout hook run.

- check helpers: hooks.check_hooks (~294) and skills.check_synced_skills return
  the same list[tuple[Path, str]] "mismatches" shape; cli renders both the same way.

PLAN:
1. Add a shared module (e.g. src/basicly/projection.py) with:
   - one `SyncResult(written, unchanged)` dataclass,
   - `write_if_changed(path, content)` — DECIDE the comparison semantics up front
     (see gotcha) and use it everywhere,
   - `report_sync(result, repo_root, *, noun)` and
     `report_mismatches(mismatches, repo_root, *, rebuild_hint)` for the CLI.
2. Point skills.py, hooks.py, and the init/build paths at the shared SyncResult +
   write_if_changed; delete HookSyncResult/SkillSyncResult and the per-file
   _write_if_changed variants.
3. Collapse cmd_hooks_build/check and cmd_skills_build/check onto the shared
   report helpers (keep their distinct nouns/hints).

GOTCHA TO RESOLVE FIRST (the crux): comparison semantics differ today — hooks
compares/writes BYTES (CRLF-sensitive, hook scripts) while skills/cli/init use
TEXT (str, newline-normalizing). Unifying naively could flip a hook file's
line-ending handling. Decide one contract (recommend: bytes-exact compare, write
bytes; callers pass encoded content) or keep a text/bytes parameter, and add a
regression test for CRLF so a hook file is not spuriously rewritten.

VERIFY (dogfood this repo): basicly build + check (no diff), skills-build +
skills-check, hooks-build + hooks-check, catalog-lint, and the full
ruff/pyright/pytest gate. Confirm each from its explicit PASS line and exit code
— do not infer success from a truncated tail.

GUARDRAILS: commit SUBJECT = lowercase letters/digits/spaces/hyphens only, NO
'.'/','/punctuation-end; bead id is a TRAILING BODY line (basicly-zrj.4.1). Run
`git status` before `git add` (pre-commit stashes unstaged tracked files, so
commit a self-consistent set). Close the bead folded into the resolving commit.
Note: another agent may be editing quality-gate.fragment.yaml — reconcile with
`br sync --merge` / rebase if there is a collision on .beads or that fragment.

ENTRY COMMANDS:
  git pull --ff-only
  br update basicly-zrj.4.1 --status in_progress --assignee visicni

title: Migrate skill source format from SKILL.md to a non-discoverable format
description: §4.2/§11.7: catalog skills authored as SKILL.md risk double-loading. Decided fix: author skills in a non-SKILL.md format and project to SKILL.md only at target roots.
design: Umbrella for the skills half: 9wl (schema plus loader) and w39 (projector) land together so skills-build never breaks between them. Part of epic basicly-c1c.
acceptance_criteria: Skills authored as skill.yaml and projected to SKILL.md at target roots only; the double-load source is removed; skills-check and full gate pass.
close_reason: Skill source format migrated SKILL.md -> skill.yaml, projected to SKILL.md at target roots only; double-load source removed; skills-check + full gate (241 tests) pass.

title: Post-release distribution backlog (deferred in §11.8-11.9)
description: Captured so nothing is lost; explicitly out of scope for v0.1 per architecture §11.8-11.9.
close_reason: epic complete: technology scoping (aff) and bootstrap shim (hmc) shipped; codex scoped rules (30j) and cursor target (3ii) closed as wont-do pending real consumer demand

title: hooks projection rewrites/compares whole pre-commit config instead of its managed block
description: Review findings 1+2: check_hooks compares full file text against a yaml.safe_dump re-render, so any hand-formatted config (including this repo's own dogfooded one — verified, exits 1) is permanently 'stale'; hooks-build round-trips the whole file through safe_load/safe_dump, destroying comments and reformatting foreign blocks, contradicting the 'never clobbered' contract. Fix: compare managed hooks semantically (parse + subset match by id); rewrite the file only when a managed hook is actually missing/wrong; warn when a rewrite normalizes formatting. Also validate hooks.yaml entries with a clear error instead of a bare KeyError.
close_reason: hooks projection now compares/edits only its managed hooks semantically: managed_hook_mismatches() parses the config and subset-matches each managed hook by id (formatting/comments/block-grouping agnostic); sync_hooks rewrites the co-owned file only when a managed hook is missing/wrong, with a CLI note when a rewrite normalizes formatting; invalid YAML raises a clear error; hooks.yaml entries validated (no bare KeyError). Regression tests: dogfood config passes its own gate (was exit 1), comments survive no-op sync, tampered entry detected+repaired. 67 tests pass.

title: init hardcodes .basicly/core and default-config literal duplicates config.py defaults
description: Review findings 3+4+10: cmd_init materializes into a hardcoded .basicly/core while build/hooks-build honor basicly.toml — with a custom core path, init->build silently generates nothing (reproduced). _core_hooks_dir also derives hooks from core_fragments.parent inconsistently with init. Fix: single core_root notion on ProjectPaths used by init and hooks; guard against absolute core paths leaking into committed config; move the scaffolded basicly.toml default into config.py next to the path defaults with a test asserting they agree.
close_reason: cmd_init now materializes into ProjectPaths.core_root (derived once from core_fragments.parent) instead of a hardcoded .basicly/core; _core_hooks_dir uses the same notion and rejects absolute paths (would bake machine-specific paths into the shared pre-commit config). The scaffolded basicly.toml moved to config.DEFAULT_CONFIG_TOML next to the built-in defaults with a test asserting parse-equality. Regression test reproduces the custom-path consumer flow: init -> build -> hooks-build all target the configured root. 70 tests pass.

title: pre-commit entry string is built unquoted — paths with spaces break every commit
description: Review finding 5: _hook_entry f-string concatenates the script path into 'entry' unquoted; pre-commit shlex-splits entry, so a core path containing a space yields a hook that fails on every commit. Fix: shlex.quote the script path.
close_reason: shlex.quote the script path inside the pre-commit entry string; test covers space-containing path round-trip and that plain paths stay unquoted (dogfood config semantics unchanged — live hooks-check still passes).

title: Spike: evaluate basicly harness against MS AI Foundry production-agent principles
description:
Research spike (not implementation). Curated statements from Microsoft AI Foundry engineers building production agents describe the layers a production agent harness needs. basicly is our custom harness built ON TOP of code agents (Claude Code / Codex / Copilot) that already provide some of these layers. Goal: test basicly's design/architecture/engineering against each principle, mapping — for every dimension — (a) what the underlying code agent already provides, (b) what basicly currently does, (c) the gap, (d) concrete improvements basicly should own. File follow-up implementation beads for the accepted gaps.

Dimensions (one child task each): identity & audit trail; guardrails; observability & fleet governance; harness-as-first-class / model swappability & inference layer; agent runtime boundary; agentic context/retrieval layer; rubric-based evaluation.

Source framing to preserve: "The harness matters as much as the model" — runtime, tools, context retrieval, identity, guardrails, evaluators, deployment pipeline; models are swappable and each has different properties the harness must adjust to. "Identity bounds what an agent is allowed to reach; guardrails bound what flows through it once it acts."

## Acceptance Criteria

- Each child produces a written gap analysis (agent-provides / basicly-does / gap / recommended improvements) committed to docs or tracker comments; prioritized implementation beads filed for accepted gaps; a short synthesis rolls the seven dimensions into a basicly harness-maturity view.
close_reason: shipped by the harness loop

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Synthesis (docs/foundry-spike.md -> Synthesis section). Headline: basicly is a strong, correctly-scoped WORKFLOW harness with a THIN runtime-governance layer. It governs the commit boundary (post-action, deterministic) far better than the action boundary (in-flight, per-agent, per-model). Maturity: D5 runtime-boundary Strong (delegates correctly); D6 retrieval / D1 identity / D4 model Partial; D3 observability / D7 rubric-eval / D2 guardrails Thin. Keystone: a structured runner run-record (basicly-z6dh) is the single highest-leverage change - four dimensions (D1/D3/D4/D5) converge on it. Weakest axis: identity+guardrails (its strongest in-flight control, the deny-list, is not even shipped to consumers). 13 implementation beads filed; recommended order: z6dh -> u0zg+yzyd -> 0122 -> P2/P3 build-outs. Wontfix kept honest: token-level inference client, agentic retrieval loop, raw token re-metering, portable per-agent role enforcement.

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: [workstream] Foundry gap follow-ons — where to start next session. Recommended start: basicly-45ld (first-class model field + provenance; 140a/lqz5/t0kt build on the same runner seam). Hard constraints for this workstream: (1) isolate parallel/non-trivial work via loop git WORKTREES not subagent_type:fork (forks inherit the full directive and one drove a whole loop); (2) run every 'basicly loop advance' from the BASE checkout on main, never cd into the worktree — edit worktree files by absolute path; (3) commit tracker state in base before advance provisions a worktree, never commit .beads/issues.jsonl on a harness branch; (4) use the conventional-commits skill for every commit subject (no commas/dots/underscores/capitals) and put node on PATH before committing/pushing (WSL nvm quirk: /home/niksa/.nvm/versions/node/v26.5.0/bin); (5) for bug/chore/task DoR add ## Acceptance Criteria (and ## Steps to Reproduce for bugs) before classify; (6) VERIFY BY EXERCISING the change (run it, read output), not just tests; do not push unless asked.

title: Foundry spike: identity & audit trail for harness-driven agent actions
description:
Principle: without identity controls an agent runs as a shared system principal with no audit trail; agents need their own identities, role assignments, and audit trails, bounded like a misbehaving employee. Analyze: harness loop actions (worktree create/merge/push, br claims/closes, commits) currently run under the human developer git identity. What attribution/audit exists (beads external-ref, commit trailers, br assignee)? Gap vs per-agent identity + scoped roles + tamper-evident audit. What is the code agent vs basicly responsibility.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Gap analysis (agent-provides/basicly-does/gap/recommended); implementation beads filed for accepted gaps.
close_reason: done

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Gap analysis committed: docs/foundry-spike.md -> Dimension 1 (Identity & audit trail). Verdict Partial: basicly guards the absence of identity but has no per-agent identity/attribution. Follow-up beads: basicly-140a (P2 attribute agent/model on commits+gates), basicly-smzg (P3 per-agent bot identity + tamper-evidence). Role enforcement is wontfix here (agent/OS trust boundary; projectable slice folds into D2).

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Gap analysis delivered in docs/foundry-spike.md — Dimension 1 (Identity & audit trail). Accepted-gap beads: basicly-140a (P2), basicly-smzg (P3). Wontfix: portable per-agent role enforcement (agent/OS trust boundary).

title: Foundry spike: guardrails on what flows through the agent when it acts
description:
Principle: identity bounds what an agent can reach; guardrails bound what flows through it once it acts (stop it confidently doing/saying what it should not). Map basicly current guardrails: deterministic verify/lint/type gates, commit-msg + beads-commit-msg hooks, protect-generated hook, .claude/settings.json deny-list, runner dry-run. Gap vs output/content guardrails, tool-call allow/deny at runtime, egress/network controls, secret redaction.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Gap analysis (agent-provides/basicly-does/gap/recommended); implementation beads filed for accepted gaps.
close_reason: done

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Gap analysis committed: docs/foundry-spike.md -> Dimension 2 (Guardrails). Verdict Thin (highest-signal): guardrails are mostly post-action commit gates; the strong deny-list is repo-local and NOT projected to consumers. Follow-up beads: basicly-u0zg (P1 project deny-list across agents), basicly-yzyd (P1 secret-scan gate), basicly-3p2i (P2 runner output redaction + egress), basicly-yw28 (P3 protect-generated git-stage backstop).

title: Foundry spike: observability & fleet governance (tokens, latency, health, drift, rollups)
description:
Principle: a single view of every agent across every project with health scoring, token usage, latency, drift detection, and cross-project rollups; without it regressions are invisible and cost uncontrolled. Map basicly current: tool-usage telemetry (basicly usage report), basicly status --json, the dev cross-project workspace. Gap vs per-agent health/token/latency metrics, drift detection, and a fleet rollup across housed repos.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Gap analysis (agent-provides/basicly-does/gap/recommended); implementation beads filed for accepted gaps.

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Gap analysis committed: docs/foundry-spike.md -> Dimension 3 (Observability & fleet). Verdict Thin: no per-run token/latency/health metric; drift means config drift; no fleet rollup. Follow-up beads: basicly-z6dh (P1 runner run-record, foundational/cross-cutting), basicly-h0f0 (P2 fleet rollup), basicly-y886 (P3 health scoring + drift). Wontfix: re-metering raw per-session tokens (consume the agent's OTEL/usage).

title: Foundry spike: harness-as-first-class, model swappability & inference layer
description:
Principle: the harness matters as much as the model; the inference layer is one interface to swappable models, and each model has different properties the harness must adjust to (unlike DB versions). Map basicly current: agent-agnostic runner adapters (claude/codex/copilot + manual), config-driven. Gap vs a single inference interface, model-version/property adjustment, and capability probing beyond PATH detection.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Gap analysis (agent-provides/basicly-does/gap/recommended); implementation beads filed for accepted gaps.
close_reason: done

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Gap analysis committed: docs/foundry-spike.md -> Dimension 4 (Model swappability & inference layer). Verdict Partial: basicly swaps the agent binary, not the model, and adjusts to neither; RunnerSpec has no model field; capability detection is PATH-presence only. Follow-up beads: basicly-45ld (P2 first-class model field + provenance), basicly-bveo (P2 capability probe beyond PATH). Wontfix: token-level inference client and model-derived caps (until a consumer needs them).

title: Foundry spike: agent runtime boundary (orchestration loop, tool calls, state, protocol)
description:
Principle: the agent runtime turns a model into an agent — orchestration loop, tool calls, conversation state, the protocol the rest of the harness speaks. Most of this is the code agent job (Claude Code), not basicly. Clarify the boundary: basicly loop is the WORKFLOW runtime (phases/gates/worktrees), not the token-level runtime. Identify what basicly adds vs delegates and where the protocol seam is.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Written boundary map (agent-runtime vs harness-workflow-runtime); any seam-hardening beads filed.

comments.text: Boundary map committed: docs/foundry-spike.md -> Dimension 5 (Agent runtime boundary). Verdict Strong/delegates correctly: basicly is the coarse resumable workflow runtime (phases/gates/worktrees/merge-queue, durable state in br via derive_phase); the code agent owns the token-level runtime; the runner adapter is the whole protocol seam (static tracker-anchored prompt in, RunResult out). No unique bead: the only thin spot (bare exit code out) folds into basicly-z6dh (run-record) + basicly-o774 (structured needs-input).

title: Foundry spike: agentic context/retrieval layer with structured I-dont-know
description:
Principle: wrap retrieval in an agentic loop — plan sources, execute queries, evaluate against the question, decide return/refine/try-another; when iteration runs out return a structured I-dont-know instead of a confident wrong answer. Map basicly current: static projection of fragments/skills/AGENTS.md, knowledge-priming rule, skills progressive disclosure. Gap: context is static projection, not an agentic retrieval loop; evaluate whether a retrieval agent belongs in the harness or the code agent.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Gap analysis (agent-provides/basicly-does/gap/recommended); implementation beads filed for accepted gaps.
close_reason: done

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Gap analysis committed: docs/foundry-spike.md -> Dimension 6 (Context/retrieval). Verdict Partial: context is static projection + progressive disclosure + soft prose I-dont-know; the agentic retrieval loop already lives in the code agent (wontfix to duplicate). basicly's real gap is the structured I-dont-know. Follow-up bead: basicly-o774 (P2 structured I-dont-know / needs-input as a first-class loop outcome; shared with D5).

title: Foundry spike: rubric-based evaluation of agent behaviors
description:
Principle: evaluate the agent against specific use-case-tied behaviors via rubrics (yes/no checks about what the agent should be doing), not just generic metrics. Map basicly current: deterministic gates + advisory semantic review (basicly review) + catalog-verify. Gap vs a rubric framework (use-case-tied yes/no checks) wired into verify/review; relation to the loop gate ledger (required vs advisory).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Gap analysis (agent-provides/basicly-does/gap/recommended); a rubric-eval design proposal + implementation beads filed for accepted gaps.
close_reason: done

comments.author: niksa
comments.text: Gap analysis + design proposal committed: docs/foundry-spike.md -> Dimension 7 (Rubric evaluation). Verdict Thin: deterministic gates check artifacts generically, advisory review is a fixed catalog prompt; no use-case-tied yes/no behavioral rubrics. Design: rubrics as catalog source (yaml), deterministic-first/agent-judged, reported as a br gate that is advisory by default and promotable to required per work-type. Follow-up bead: basicly-0122 (P1 rubric-eval framework).

title: Docs: note the br probe-leaves-tombstone trap in tool-br guidance
description:
Retro finding from the 2026-07-18 foundry-followon session (basicly-58iu). While investigating br behavior (what `br lint` flags, a `br show --json` key name), throwaway beads were created and `br delete`d. `br delete` leaves a TOMBSTONE row in `.beads/issues.jsonl`, which the harness loop's tracker-state commit then captures — so junk probe beads reach the project tracker history unless pruned. `--ephemeral` is not a workaround: ephemeral beads do not export to JSONL but `br lint` also will not lint them (`total:0`), so a lint probe needs a real bead.

Put this guidance where the next agent authoring br probes will see it: add a short note to the `tool-br` skill (or a quirks fragment) covering the trap and the avoidance. There is no `br compact`/`purge`/`gc`; the only clean prune is `br delete <id> --hard --force` ("prunes tombstones from JSONL immediately").

## Acceptance Criteria

- The `tool-br` skill (or a quirks fragment) gains a concise note: investigating br by creating+deleting real beads leaves tombstones the loop commits; prune with `br delete --hard`, or avoid probing the live tracker; ephemeral beads are not linted.
- Authored as YAML catalog source and projected (`basicly skills-build`/`build`); `basicly catalog lint` and the relevant `*-check` gate pass.
- Fits within the always-on budget if placed in an applies_to:[all] fragment (prefer the on-demand tool-br skill to avoid baseline growth).

title: Slim always-on instruction files: collapse skill-duplicating fragments to pointers
description: Apply progressive-disclosure/instruction-budget principles (writing-a-good-claude.md research): collapse harness-loop, self-improvement-retro, session-completion fragments to skill-pointers; move needs-input.json protocol into harness-loop skill; make session-finish skill self-canonical; scope quirks off the Claude always-on wrapper. Slims CLAUDE.md 162->148, AGENTS.md 168->160; all projection gates green.
close_reason: done

title: Tech-specific skill taxonomy + spec-compliant rich-skill engine
description:
Goal: extract tech-specific and situational guidance out of the always-on instruction files into real Agent Skills, using basicly's existing 'technologies' scoping (architecture §9: untagged = universal/core, always ships; tagged e.g. technologies:[python] = optional, ships only when the consuming repo selects it via [catalog] technologies in basicly.toml). Keep ALL current projection outputs unchanged (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, .claude/rules/, plus skills to BOTH .claude/skills [Claude] and .agents/skills [Codex/Copilot/Cursor]). Also upgrade the skill engine to the FULL Agent Skills spec so skills can bundle references/scripts/assets and any additional files.

Background/decisions (from design session):
- Claude Code reads only CLAUDE.md (not AGENTS.md) and discovers skills only from .claude/skills; Codex/Copilot/Cursor read AGENTS.md and .agents/skills. So both skill roots stay mandatory; no always-on file is dropped.
- Enforcement stays deterministic in hooks (ruff/pytest); skills carry judgment + pointers ('never send an LLM to do a linter's job').
- Quirks are NOT a skill; each quirk is distributed into the skill that owns its domain.

Specs (authoritative):
- Agent Skills: https://agentskills.io/specification
- AGENTS.md: https://agents.md/
- Skills tooling + agent discovery paths: https://github.com/vercel-labs/skills/blob/main/README.md
- Exemplar AGENTS.md: https://github.com/vercel-labs/skills/blob/main/AGENTS.md

Children decompose this; see each child for exact files + acceptance criteria.

title: Rich-skill engine: full Agent Skills spec compliance (bundle references/scripts/assets + extended frontmatter)
description:
WHY: basicly currently authors a skill as .basicly/core/skills/<name>/skill.yaml (fields: name, description, instructions, technologies) and renders ONLY a single SKILL.md via skills.py:render_skill_md/sync_skills. It does NOT copy references/, scripts/, assets/, or any additional files to the skill roots. The Agent Skills spec (https://agentskills.io/specification) allows a skill directory to contain SKILL.md (required) plus scripts/, references/, assets/, and '... Any additional files or directories'. To author powerful skills (e.g. a python skill with a long references/ style guide + a fixer scripts/), the engine must project the whole skill directory.

SCOPE / FILES:
- src/basicly/skills.py: sync_skills / check_synced_skills / discover_skills / render_skill_md — copy every file & subdirectory of the source skill dir (except the skill.yaml source, which renders to SKILL.md) into each destination root (.claude/skills/<name>/ and .agents/skills/<name>/), verbatim, preserving structure. Prune on technology deselection. Add/verify freshness in check.
- src/basicly/schema.py + .basicly/core/schemas/skill.schema.json: extend the skill source to support the spec's optional frontmatter passthrough — license, compatibility, metadata (map), allowed-tools — rendered into SKILL.md frontmatter. Keep 'technologies' (basicly-internal scoping; NOT emitted into SKILL.md frontmatter, or emitted under metadata — decide and document).
- Generated-marker / protect-generated hooks (.basicly/core/hooks/protect-generated*.py): SKILL.md stays generated (marker), but bundled reference/script/asset files are HAND-AUTHORED sources that must be copied but NOT carry the generated marker and NOT be clobbered/pruned wrongly. Define the rule and enforce it.
- .basicly/generated-manifest.json: track projected skill files (including copied resources) so build/check/uninstall stay consistent.
- catalog-lint (.basicly/core/hooks/catalog-lint.py + src/basicly/catalog_lint.py): validate skill dirs against the spec — name matches dir (1-64 chars, lowercase a-z0-9 and single hyphens, no leading/trailing/consecutive hyphen), description non-empty <=1024 chars, SKILL.md body kept small (warn > ~500 lines per spec progressive disclosure), file references one level deep.
- basicly build / skills-build / skills-check / install / uninstall: end-to-end still green with resource files present.
- docs/architecture.md: document the full-spec skill layout and the source->projection mapping.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
1. A skill authored as .basicly/core/skills/<name>/ with skill.yaml + references/REF.md + scripts/x.sh + assets/y + an arbitrary extra file/dir projects ALL of them (unchanged bytes) into BOTH .claude/skills/<name>/ and .agents/skills/<name>/, alongside the rendered SKILL.md.
2. Optional frontmatter (license, compatibility, metadata, allowed-tools) round-trips from skill.yaml into SKILL.md frontmatter per spec; omitting them yields today's minimal frontmatter.
3. Technology deselection prunes the whole projected skill dir (all resources), not just SKILL.md.
4. basicly check / skills-check --all-default-roots / hooks-check / catalog lint all pass; protect-generated does not flag hand-authored resource files, and does not allow manual edits to generated SKILL.md.
5. New unit tests cover: full-dir copy, extra '...' files, frontmatter passthrough, prune-on-deselect, and check-detects-drift for a resource file.
6. 'skills-ref validate ./<projected-skill>' (https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills/tree/main/skills-ref) passes for a projected skill, or a documented reason if that tool is unavailable in-repo.

SPEC: https://agentskills.io/specification (directory structure incl. the '...' line; frontmatter table; progressive disclosure: metadata ~100 tokens, SKILL.md < ~5000 tokens / < 500 lines, resources on demand; file references one level deep; validation via skills-ref).

title: Convert test-discipline fragment to a universal (core) skill
description:
Convert .basicly/core/fragments/testing/test-discipline.fragment.yaml (currently a scoped fragment -> .claude/rules/test-discipline.md, inlined into AGENTS.md) into a UNIVERSAL skill .basicly/core/skills/test-discipline/skill.yaml (NO technologies tag => always ships). Content is tech-agnostic testing hygiene (isolation, no shared mutable state / order-independence, assert on observable behavior not internals). Give it a strong what+when description. Delete the fragment after.
ACCEPTANCE: skill present in .claude/skills + .agents/skills; '## Test Discipline' gone from AGENTS.md; .claude/rules/test-discipline.md removed; gates green. Depends on engine (basicly-q1w9.1) so it is authored to the final skill schema. SPEC: https://agentskills.io/specification

title: Author the python optional skill (technologies: [python])
description:
Create .basicly/core/skills/python/skill.yaml with name: python, technologies: [python], and a strong what+when description (creating/editing .py, type hints, pathlib, subprocess/shell-out on Windows/POSIX). Merge in: (a) python-style fragment content (type hints; prefer pathlib; formatting ENFORCED by ruff/pre-commit — point at the hook, do not restate as prose); (b) python-domain quirks from the quirks fragment: POSIX shlex.split backslash mangles Windows paths -> use Path(...).as_posix() or an argv list; test subprocess helpers must inherit os.environ (keep PATH) or Windows CI fails with [WinError 2]. Use references/ for any longer style guide (engine bead enables this). Then DELETE .basicly-local/fragments/user/code-style/python-style.fragment.yaml.
ACCEPTANCE: python skill in both roots; '## Python Style' gone from AGENTS.md; .claude/rules/python-style.md removed; ships to basicly (which selects python) but NOT to a repo selecting only e.g. go; gates green. Depends on engine (basicly-q1w9.1). SPEC: https://agentskills.io/specification

title: Author a real, useful node skill (technologies: [node])
description:
Create .basicly/core/skills/node/skill.yaml (name: node, technologies: [node]) — a genuinely useful Node/npm skill for this ecosystem, not just a quirk dump. Include: the WSL/node trap from the quirks fragment (non-interactive WSL shells skip the profile that loads nvm, so npx/node resolve to a Windows install and break node-based hooks like markdownlint — put the repo's node version on PATH before committing/pushing from scripts and background jobs). Add practical node/npm usage guidance relevant to basicly's node use (markdownlint hook, package.json). Strong what+when description. Use references/ for depth.
ACCEPTANCE: node skill in both roots; ships when repo selects node; gates green. Depends on engine (basicly-q1w9.1). Coordinate with quirks-dissolution issue. SPEC: https://agentskills.io/specification

title: Author a real, useful WSL skill + add 'wsl' to the technology vocabulary
description:
Create .basicly/core/skills/wsl/skill.yaml — a genuinely useful WSL skill: configuring WSL, common wsl.exe commands, interop/PATH gotchas, filesystem/perf notes, and how non-interactive WSL shells differ from login shells (ties into the node/nvm trap). Then basicly build; basicly skills-build --all-default-roots; run gates: basicly check, agents-check, skills-check --all-default-roots, hooks-check, catalog lint.
